entities.displayName: Poor People's Campaign and Resurrection City
entities.nameLower: poor people's campaign and resurrection city
entities.summary: Organized by SCLC under Ralph Abernathy after Martin Luther King Jr.'s assassination, this 1968 campaign for economic justice built a 3,000-person encampment called Resurrection City on the National Mall, occupying 16 acres between the Lincoln Memorial and Washington Monument for 42 days.

claims.object: 16 acres of the National Mall with a 3,000-resident encampment called Resurrection City, complete with its own zip code, dining tent, and city hall
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Resurrection City

claims.object: Ralph Abernathy, who carried out the campaign SCLC had organized under Martin Luther King Jr. before his April 1968 assassination
claims.citationLabel: Smithsonian Magazine: Remembering Resurrection City and the Poor People's Campaign of 1968

entities.locationLabel: Resurrection City encampment, National Mall between the Lincoln Memorial and Washington Monument
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: occupied: 16 acres of the National Mall with a 3,000-resident encampment called Resurrection City, complete with its own zip code, dining tent, and city hall. Cited from nps.gov.
led by: Ralph Abernathy, who carried out the campaign SCLC had organized under Martin Luther King Jr. before his April 1968 assassination. Cited from smithsonianmag.com.

entities.historicalContext: Weeks of rain turned the encampment to mud, and thousands of police evacuated the site on June 24, 1968, the day after SCLC's National Park Service permit expired, but the campaign's Solidarity Day drew tens of thousands to the Mall in support.

entities.summary: On February 8, 1968, highway patrolmen opened fire on Black students protesting segregation at a local bowling alley on the campus of South Carolina State College, killing Samuel Hammond, Henry Smith, and Delano Middleton and wounding twenty-eight others — the first fatal police shooting of student protesters on a U.S. campus.

claims.object: February 8, 1968, when nine state troopers and one city officer fired on student protesters at South Carolina State College, killing three and wounding twenty-eight
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Orangeburg Massacre (1968)

claims.object: Samuel Hammond (18), Henry Smith (18), and Delano Middleton (17), a local high school student, while federal charges against the officers later ended in acquittal
claims.citationLabel: Smithsonian Magazine: In 1968, Three Students Were Killed by Police

entities.locationLabel: South Carolina State University, 300 College Street NE, Orangeburg
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: occurred on: February 8, 1968, when nine state troopers and one city officer fired on student protesters at South Carolina State College, killing three and wounding twenty-eight. Cited from blackpast.org.

killed: Samuel Hammond (18), Henry Smith (18), and Delano Middleton (17), a local high school student, while federal charges against the officers later ended in acquittal. Cited from smithsonianmag.com.

entities.historicalContext: SNCC organizer Cleveland Sellers, present at the protests, was the only person convicted in connection with the events — on a riot charge tied to unrest two days earlier — and was later pardoned by South Carolina in 1993.

entities.summary: Formed in 1965 by local Black residents under John Hulett and SNCC organizer Stokely Carmichael, the Lowndes County Freedom Organization ran independent Black candidates under a black panther emblem in a county nicknamed 'Bloody Lowndes' for its history of racial terror, directly inspiring the Black Panther Party's name.

claims.object: 1965 in Lowndes County, Alabama, by local Black residents led by John Hulett together with SNCC staff under Stokely Carmichael, after the Selma to Montgomery march passed through the county
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia of Alabama: Lowndes County Freedom Organization

claims.object: a black panther as its ballot emblem, in contrast to the Alabama Democratic Party's white rooster, a symbol that directly inspired Huey Newton and Bobby Seale's naming of the Black Panther Party
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Lowndes County Freedom Organization

entities.locationLabel: Hayneville, Lowndes County, Alabama
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded in: 1965 in Lowndes County, Alabama, by local Black residents led by John Hulett together with SNCC staff under Stokely Carmichael, after the Selma to Montgomery march passed through the county. Cited from encyclopediaofalabama.org.

adopted symbol: a black panther as its ballot emblem, in contrast to the Alabama Democratic Party's white rooster, a symbol that directly inspired Huey Newton and Bobby Seale's naming of the Black Panther Party. Cited from blackpast.org.

entities.historicalContext: Despite registering nearly as many Black voters as white voters by the November 1966 election, every Freedom Organization candidate lost, reflecting the continued grip of white political control even after the Voting Rights Act of 1965.

entities.displayName: Freedom Summer and the Burning of Mount Zion Church
entities.nameLower: freedom summer and the burning of mount zion church
entities.summary: Mount Zion United Methodist Church in rural Longdale, Mississippi, agreed to host a Freedom Summer Freedom School in 1964; Klansmen beat congregants and burned the church on June 16, and when civil rights workers Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner came to investigate on June 21, they were murdered nearby.

claims.object: June 16, 1964, when Klansmen assaulted Mt. Zion congregants leaving a meeting and later burned the church, which had agreed to host a Freedom Summer Freedom School
claims.citationLabel: SAH Archipedia: Mt. Zion United Methodist Church

claims.object: the June 21, 1964 murders of civil rights workers James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner, who were killed after visiting the burned church site to investigate
claims.citationLabel: Mississippi Encyclopedia: Neshoba County Murders

entities.locationLabel: Mount Zion United Methodist Church, 11191 County Road 747, Longdale community, Neshoba County
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: burned on: June 16, 1964, when Klansmen assaulted Mt. Zion congregants leaving a meeting and later burned the church, which had agreed to host a Freedom Summer Freedom School. Cited from sah-archipedia.org.

linked to: the June 21, 1964 murders of civil rights workers James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner, who were killed after visiting the burned church site to investigate. Cited from mississippiencyclopedia.org.

entities.historicalContext: The Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) coordinated Freedom Summer's voter registration drives and roughly 40 Freedom Schools across Mississippi; the congregation rebuilt Mount Zion on the same site in 1966, and it has hosted annual commemorations of the three slain workers ever since.

entities.displayName: Integration of the University of Mississippi
entities.nameLower: integration of the university of mississippi
entities.summary: James Meredith's court-ordered enrollment triggered the Ole Miss riot of September 30-October 1, 1962, when a mob of roughly 2,500 segregationists battled federal marshals near the Lyceum, killing two civilians before 30,000 federal troops restored order and Meredith registered as the university's first Black student.

claims.object: the night of September 30-October 1, 1962, when a mob of about 2,500 attacked federal marshals guarding James Meredith near the Lyceum, killing French journalist Paul Guihard and local resident Ray Gunter
claims.citationLabel: HISTORY: Riots erupt over desegregation of Ole Miss

claims.object: James Meredith's registration on October 1, 1962, as the first African American student at the University of Mississippi, after President Kennedy mobilized more than 30,000 troops
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Marshals Service: The U.S. Marshals and the Integration of the University of Mississippi

entities.locationLabel: The Lyceum, University of Mississippi, Oxford
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: occurred on: the night of September 30-October 1, 1962, when a mob of about 2,500 attacked federal marshals guarding James Meredith near the Lyceum, killing French journalist Paul Guihard and local resident Ray Gunter. Cited from history.com.

resulted in: James Meredith's registration on October 1, 1962, as the first African American student at the University of Mississippi, after President Kennedy mobilized more than 30,000 troops. Cited from usmarshals.gov.

entities.historicalContext: A statue of James Meredith and a Civil Rights Memorial now stand near the Lyceum, marking the university's own reckoning with the 1962 crisis, which remains the largest federal deployment for a single domestic disturbance in U.S. history.

entities.summary: On June 11, 1963, Governor George Wallace stood in the doorway of Foster Auditorium at the University of Alabama to block the enrollment of Vivian Malone and James Hood, stepping aside only after President Kennedy federalized the Alabama National Guard that same day.

claims.object: June 11, 1963, when Governor George Wallace stood in the doorway of Foster Auditorium to block Vivian Malone and James Hood from registering
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia of Alabama: Stand in the Schoolhouse Door

claims.object: President Kennedy's Executive Order 11111 federalizing the Alabama National Guard, whose commanding general ordered Wallace to step aside so Malone and Hood could register
claims.citationLabel: NAACP Legal Defense Fund: Fifty Years Ago, the Stand in the Schoolhouse Door

entities.locationLabel: Foster Auditorium, 801 6th Avenue, University of Alabama, Tuscaloosa
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: occurred on: June 11, 1963, when Governor George Wallace stood in the doorway of Foster Auditorium to block Vivian Malone and James Hood from registering. Cited from encyclopediaofalabama.org.

ended by: President Kennedy's Executive Order 11111 federalizing the Alabama National Guard, whose commanding general ordered Wallace to step aside so Malone and Hood could register. Cited from naacpldf.org.

entities.historicalContext: Vivian Malone went on to become the University of Alabama's first Black graduate in 1965; that evening, President Kennedy delivered a nationally televised civil rights address that helped set the stage for the Civil Rights Act of 1964.

entities.displayName: Autherine Lucy's Enrollment at the University of Alabama
entities.nameLower: autherine lucy's enrollment at the university of alabama
entities.summary: Autherine Lucy became the University of Alabama's first Black student on February 3, 1956, but after mobs rioted against her for three days, the university suspended and then expelled her, a wrong it did not formally reverse until 1988, allowing her to complete her degree in 1992.

claims.object: February 3, 1956, becoming the first African American student at the University of Alabama, before mobs chased and attacked her over the following days
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia of Alabama: Autherine Lucy

claims.object: 1988, when the university's board voted to lift her expulsion; she returned to complete her degree, graduating in 1992 alongside her daughter
claims.citationLabel: University of Alabama News: Autherine Lucy Foster - The Life of a Legend

entities.locationLabel: University of Alabama campus, Tuscaloosa
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: readmitted in: 1988, when the university's board voted to lift her expulsion; she returned to complete her degree, graduating in 1992 alongside her daughter. Cited from news.ua.edu.

enrolled on: February 3, 1956, becoming the first African American student at the University of Alabama, before mobs chased and attacked her over the following days. Cited from encyclopediaofalabama.org.

entities.historicalContext: The University of Alabama's 1956 mob violence against Lucy remains, by scholarly consensus, the most violent anti-integration response to any post-Brown v. Board college desegregation, foreshadowing the federal confrontation that would return to the same campus in 1963.

entities.displayName: Integration of the University of Georgia
entities.nameLower: integration of the university of georgia
entities.summary: Federal judge W.A. Bootle ordered the University of Georgia to admit Charlayne Hunter and Hamilton Holmes on January 6, 1961; after they registered January 9, a rioting mob smashed windows at Hunter's dormitory on January 11, but both students returned days later under a new court order and graduated.

claims.object: January 6, 1961, when Federal District Judge William A. Bootle ordered the immediate admission of Hamilton Holmes and Charlayne Hunter, ending 160 years of segregation at the university
claims.citationLabel: NAACP Legal Defense Fund: 55th Anniversary of the Desegregation of the University of Georgia

claims.object: January 11, 1961, when a crowd gathered outside Hunter's dormitory after a basketball game and smashed windows with bottles and bricks, prompting the state patrol to escort both students home before a new court order returned them to campus
claims.citationLabel: The Red & Black: Reflecting on the pioneers who desegregated UGA 60 years ago

entities.locationLabel: University of Georgia campus (The Arch and North Campus), Athens
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: ordered on: January 6, 1961, when Federal District Judge William A. Bootle ordered the immediate admission of Hamilton Holmes and Charlayne Hunter, ending 160 years of segregation at the university. Cited from naacpldf.org.

attacked on: January 11, 1961, when a crowd gathered outside Hunter's dormitory after a basketball game and smashed windows with bottles and bricks, prompting the state patrol to escort both students home before a new court order returned them to campus. Cited from redandblack.com.

entities.historicalContext: Hunter graduated in 1963 and became the first Black staff writer at The New Yorker; Holmes became the first Black student admitted to Emory University's medical school the same year, and both later had campus buildings named in their honor.

entities.summary: Built in 1954 by Black businessman A.G. Gaston, this Birmingham motel served as campaign headquarters for the 1963 Birmingham movement, with King, Abernathy, and Shuttlesworth strategizing from Room 30's 'War Room' until a Klan bombing struck the motel the night after the campaign's truce was announced.

claims.object: the Birmingham campaign's headquarters, where King, Ralph Abernathy, and Fred Shuttlesworth planned strategy from Room 30, known as the 'War Room,' from April through May 1963
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: The A.G. Gaston Motel and the Birmingham Civil Rights National Monument

claims.object: May 11, 1963, the day after the truce ending the Birmingham campaign was announced at the motel, in a bombing believed carried out by Klan members cooperating with Birmingham police
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: The A.G. Gaston Motel and the Birmingham Civil Rights National Monument

entities.locationLabel: A.G. Gaston Motel, 1510 5th Avenue North, Birmingham
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: served as: the Birmingham campaign's headquarters, where King, Ralph Abernathy, and Fred Shuttlesworth planned strategy from Room 30, known as the 'War Room,' from April through May 1963. Cited from nps.gov.

bombed on: May 11, 1963, the day after the truce ending the Birmingham campaign was announced at the motel, in a bombing believed carried out by Klan members cooperating with Birmingham police. Cited from nps.gov.

entities.historicalContext: The motel was one of the only Birmingham establishments where Black travelers, entertainers, and civil rights leaders could safely lodge under segregation, making it a natural nerve center for the 1963 campaign.

entities.summary: Formed the evening of December 5, 1955 at a mass meeting of thousands at Holt Street Baptist Church, the Montgomery Improvement Association elected 26-year-old Martin Luther King Jr. as its president and organized the carpool system that sustained the 381-day Montgomery Bus Boycott.

claims.object: December 5, 1955, at a mass meeting of several thousand people at Holt Street Baptist Church following Rosa Parks' arrest
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Montgomery Improvement Association (1955-1969)

claims.object: Martin Luther King Jr., then 26 and new to the Montgomery community, chosen without opposition to lead the new organization
claims.citationLabel: Library of Congress: The Montgomery Improvement Association

entities.locationLabel: Holt Street Baptist Church, corner of Holt Street and Bullock Street, Montgomery
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded on: December 5, 1955, at a mass meeting of several thousand people at Holt Street Baptist Church following Rosa Parks' arrest. Cited from blackpast.org.
elected president: Martin Luther King Jr., then 26 and new to the Montgomery community, chosen without opposition to lead the new organization. Cited from loc.gov.

entities.historicalContext: Beyond running the boycott's carpool logistics and negotiating with city officials, the MIA taught nonviolence classes to prepare the Black community for the integrated buses that followed the Browder v. Gayle ruling.

entities.summary: Martin Luther King Jr. led the Montgomery Bus Boycott, co-founded and presided over SCLC, delivered the 'I Have a Dream' speech, and became the youngest Nobel Peace Prize laureate in 1964 before his assassination in Memphis on April 4, 1968.

claims.object: the 1964 Nobel Peace Prize at age 35, then its youngest recipient, for combating racial inequality through nonviolent resistance
claims.citationLabel: NobelPrize.org: Martin Luther King Jr. - Biographical

claims.object: April 4, 1968, shot on the balcony of the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, Tennessee, while supporting striking sanitation workers
claims.citationLabel: Britannica: Today in History, April 4 - Martin Luther King Jr. Is Assassinated

entities.locationLabel: Martin Luther King Jr. Birth Home, 501 Auburn Avenue, Atlanta
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: awarded: the 1964 Nobel Peace Prize at age 35, then its youngest recipient, for combating racial inequality through nonviolent resistance. Cited from nobelprize.org.
assassinated on: April 4, 1968, shot on the balcony of the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, Tennessee, while supporting striking sanitation workers. Cited from britannica.com.

entities.historicalContext: This entry is tied to King's birth home on Atlanta's Auburn Avenue, the best-documented site of his personal formation, since his Montgomery, Selma, Birmingham, and Memphis campaigns are each anchored to their own entries elsewhere in this catalog. He grew up in Sweet Auburn under the ministry of his father and grandfather at Ebenezer Baptist Church before leading the movement's most consequential nonviolent campaigns.

entities.summary: Born Malcolm Little, he rose to national prominence as a minister of the Nation of Islam before breaking with the group in 1964, making his hajj to Mecca as El-Hajj Malik El-Shabazz, founding the Organization of Afro-American Unity, and being assassinated at the Audubon Ballroom on February 21, 1965.

claims.object: the Organization of Afro-American Unity in June 1964, after leaving the Nation of Islam and completing his hajj to Mecca as El-Hajj Malik El-Shabazz, giving the OAAU's first public address at the Audubon Ballroom on June 28, 1964
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Malcolm X (1925-1965)

claims.object: February 21, 1965, shot while addressing an Organization of Afro-American Unity rally at the Audubon Ballroom in Manhattan
claims.citationLabel: HISTORY: Malcolm X assassinated

entities.locationLabel: Audubon Ballroom, 3940 Broadway, Washington Heights, Manhattan
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: assassinated on: February 21, 1965, shot while addressing an Organization of Afro-American Unity rally at the Audubon Ballroom in Manhattan. Cited from history.com.

founded: the Organization of Afro-American Unity in June 1964, after leaving the Nation of Islam and completing his hajj to Mecca as El-Hajj Malik El-Shabazz, giving the OAAU's first public address at the Audubon Ballroom on June 28, 1964. Cited from blackpast.org.

entities.historicalContext: This entry is tied to the Audubon Ballroom because it is where Malcolm X both launched his post-pilgrimage organizing and was killed, making it the single site most identified with his final, most internationally engaged chapter of activism; his birth site, Boston home, and Lansing childhood home each have their own entries in this catalog.

entities.summary: A behind-the-scenes strategist for more than five decades, Ella Baker directed NAACP branches nationally, served as SCLC's first executive director, and convened the 1960 Shaw University conference that founded SNCC, all while championing grassroots, group-centered leadership over charismatic figureheads.

claims.object: NAACP branch operations nationally as director of branches starting in 1943, becoming the organization's highest-ranking woman
claims.citationLabel: Britannica: Ella Baker

claims.object: the April 1960 Shaw University conference that founded SNCC, urging students to form an independent organization rather than an SCLC youth wing
claims.citationLabel: SNCC Digital Gateway: Ella Baker

entities.locationLabel: Shaw University, 118 E South Street, Raleigh
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: directed: NAACP branch operations nationally as director of branches starting in 1943, becoming the organization's highest-ranking woman. Cited from britannica.com.
convened: the April 1960 Shaw University conference that founded SNCC, urging students to form an independent organization rather than an SCLC youth wing. Cited from snccdigital.org.

entities.historicalContext: This entry is tied to Shaw University, Baker's own alma mater (valedictorian, 1927) and the site of the SNCC founding conference she organized, the single event most identified with her insistence on empowering young organizers rather than directing them herself.

entities.summary: An openly gay pacifist and organizing strategist, Bayard Rustin introduced Martin Luther King Jr. to Gandhian nonviolence, helped plan the 1947 Journey of Reconciliation and 1961 Freedom Rides, and served as chief organizer of the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom.

claims.object: the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom in under two months in 1963, bringing more than 200,000 participants to the National Mall as the event's principal organizer
claims.citationLabel: National Museum of African American History and Culture: Bayard Rustin

claims.object: a posthumous Presidential Medal of Freedom awarded November 20, 2013, and a 2020 pardon of his 1953 conviction
claims.citationLabel: National Museum of African American History and Culture: Bayard Rustin

entities.locationLabel: Lincoln Memorial, National Mall, Washington, DC
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: organized: the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom in under two months in 1963, bringing more than 200,000 participants to the National Mall as the event's principal organizer. Cited from nmaahc.si.edu.
honored with: a posthumous Presidential Medal of Freedom awarded November 20, 2013, and a 2020 pardon of his 1953 conviction. Cited from nmaahc.si.edu.

entities.historicalContext: Because of anti-gay discrimination within the movement's own ranks, Rustin often worked out of public view; this entry is tied to the Lincoln Memorial and National Mall, the site of his most consequential and best-documented organizing achievement.

entities.summary: A Mississippi sharecropper turned SNCC organizer, Fannie Lou Hamer survived a brutal jailhouse beating in Winona in 1963, co-founded the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, and delivered nationally televised 1964 Democratic National Convention testimony that exposed the violence behind Black disenfranchisement.

claims.object: the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party in 1964 as an alternative to the all-white state Democratic Party, serving as its vice president
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Fannie Lou Hamer (1917-1977)

claims.object: the 1964 Democratic National Convention Credentials Committee in Atlantic City, describing her jailhouse beating in Winona, Mississippi, in testimony that was broadcast nationally despite President Johnson's efforts to preempt it
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Fannie Lou Hamer (1917-1977)

entities.locationLabel: Ruleville, Sunflower County, Mississippi
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: co founded: the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party in 1964 as an alternative to the all-white state Democratic Party, serving as its vice president. Cited from blackpast.org.

testified at: the 1964 Democratic National Convention Credentials Committee in Atlantic City, describing her jailhouse beating in Winona, Mississippi, in testimony that was broadcast nationally despite President Johnson's efforts to preempt it. Cited from blackpast.org.

entities.historicalContext: Hamer began organizing after volunteering at a 1962 SNCC voter registration meeting in Ruleville, the Sunflower County town that remained her home and base of activism for the rest of her life.

entities.summary: As the 22-year-old chairman of the Nashville Student Movement, Diane Nash led the 1960 sit-ins that made Nashville the first Southern city to desegregate lunch counters, then insisted the Freedom Rides continue after 1961 mob violence, and later co-founded SNCC and helped drive the Selma voting-rights campaign.

claims.object: the Nashville sit-in campaign as its chairman in 1960, which made Nashville the first major Southern city to desegregate its lunch counters
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Diane Nash (1938- )

claims.object: continuing the Freedom Rides after the original riders were attacked in Alabama in May 1961, recruiting Fisk and other students to keep the rides going
claims.citationLabel: Britannica: Diane Nash

entities.locationLabel: Downtown Nashville, Tennessee
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: insisted on: continuing the Freedom Rides after the original riders were attacked in Alabama in May 1961, recruiting Fisk and other students to keep the rides going. Cited from britannica.com.
led: the Nashville sit-in campaign as its chairman in 1960, which made Nashville the first major Southern city to desegregate its lunch counters. Cited from blackpast.org.

entities.historicalContext: Nash later co-initiated the Alabama Voting Rights Project with her husband James Bevel and worked on the Selma campaign that helped secure the Voting Rights Act of 1965; President Biden awarded her the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2022.

entities.summary: A founding member and later chairman of SNCC, John Lewis was one of the original 1961 Freedom Riders and, on March 7, 1965, led marchers across the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, suffering a fractured skull on 'Bloody Sunday' before serving Georgia in Congress for over three decades.

claims.object: over 600 marchers across the Edmund Pettus Bridge on March 7, 1965, 'Bloody Sunday,' where he suffered a concussion and fractured skull from state troopers' attack
claims.citationLabel: National Archives: John Lewis

claims.object: SNCC from 1963 to 1966 and was one of the 'Big Six' leaders who organized the 1963 March on Washington
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: John Lewis (1940-2020)

entities.locationLabel: Edmund Pettus Bridge, Selma, Alabama
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: led: over 600 marchers across the Edmund Pettus Bridge on March 7, 1965, 'Bloody Sunday,' where he suffered a concussion and fractured skull from state troopers' attack. Cited from archives.gov.
chaired: SNCC from 1963 to 1966 and was one of the 'Big Six' leaders who organized the 1963 March on Washington. Cited from blackpast.org.

entities.historicalContext: Lewis went on to serve as an Atlanta city councilman and then as U.S. Representative for Georgia's 5th congressional district from 1987 until his death in 2020, becoming known as the 'conscience of the Congress.'

entities.summary: Born in Trinidad and raised in the Bronx, Stokely Carmichael organized Mississippi voter registration and the Lowndes County Freedom Organization before becoming SNCC's chairman in 1966, popularizing 'Black Power' during the Meredith March Against Fear and later adopting the name Kwame Ture as a Pan-Africanist organizer in Guinea.

claims.object: SNCC in May 1966, replacing John Lewis and signaling the organization's shift away from an emphasis on nonviolence and integration
claims.citationLabel: Britannica: Stokely Carmichael

claims.object: the phrase 'Black Power' in a speech to about 1,500 people in Greenwood, Mississippi on June 16, 1966, during the Meredith March Against Fear

entities.locationLabel: Greenwood, Mississippi
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: became chairman of: SNCC in May 1966, replacing John Lewis and signaling the organization's shift away from an emphasis on nonviolence and integration. Cited from britannica.com.
popularized: the phrase 'Black Power' in a speech to about 1,500 people in Greenwood, Mississippi on June 16, 1966, during the Meredith March Against Fear. Cited from archives.gov.

entities.historicalContext: This entry is tied to Greenwood, Mississippi, the site of the speech most associated with Carmichael's national emergence; he later changed his name to Kwame Ture and helped found the All-African People's Revolutionary Party while living in Guinea.

entities.summary: Pastor of Bethel Baptist Church and co-founder of the Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights, Fred Shuttlesworth survived a Klan dynamite attack on his parsonage on Christmas night 1956 and went on to co-found SCLC and lead the 1963 Birmingham campaign, earning King's description as the South's most courageous civil rights fighter.

claims.object: a Ku Klux Klan dynamite bombing of his parsonage next to Bethel Baptist Church on Christmas night, December 25, 1956, emerging unhurt from the rubble
claims.citationLabel: Equal Justice Initiative: Ku Klux Klan Bombs Home of Rev. Fred Shuttlesworth

claims.object: the Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights in May 1956 with Ed Gardner, to continue civil rights organizing after Alabama outlawed the NAACP from operating in the state
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: The Historic Bethel Baptist Church

entities.historicalContext: Rustin's published account of his chain-gang sentence helped inspire both Rosa Parks' 1955 refusal and the 1961 Freedom Rides that retraced much of the same route with far larger numbers and national media attention.

entities.summary: Formed in February 1962 to unite SNCC, CORE, the NAACP, and SCLC's work inside Mississippi, COFO — led by NAACP's Aaron Henry as president and SNCC's Bob Moses as program director — coordinated the state's voter-registration campaigns, the 1963 Freedom Vote, and the 1964 Freedom Summer Project.

entities.historicalContext: COFO's structure let national civil rights groups pool funding, largely from the Voter Education Project, and staff a single statewide effort rather than competing for resources, laying the organizational groundwork for the 1963 Freedom Vote and 1964 Freedom Summer.

entities.summary: Launched April 1, 1962 as a program of the Southern Regional Council under executive director Wiley Branton, the Voter Education Project funded civil rights organizations' voter-registration drives across eleven Southern states, helping register roughly 688,000 Black voters by October 1964.

entities.historicalContext: Rather than register voters directly, the Project granted funds to SNCC, CORE, the NAACP, SCLC, and the National Urban League for local registration drives, becoming the movement's central financial engine for voting-rights organizing in the early 1960s.

entities.summary: Conceived by Allard Lowenstein and Bob Moses, the 1963 Freedom Vote had roughly 80,000 disenfranchised Black Mississippians cast ballots in a parallel mock election for an integrated Aaron Henry-Edwin King ticket, proving Black political engagement and laying groundwork for the following year's Freedom Summer.

claims.object: COFO, with Bob Moses running the in-state campaign and Allard Lowenstein handling outside publicity, fundraising, and recruitment of roughly 70 Yale and Stanford student volunteers

entities.historicalContext: The Freedom Vote's demonstration that Black Mississippians would turn out in large numbers if given a fair chance to vote directly convinced COFO organizers to recruit hundreds of out-of-state volunteers for the far larger 1964 Freedom Summer Project.

entities.summary: Formed in Jonesboro, Louisiana in July 1964 after armed Klan motorcades paraded through a Black neighborhood, the Deacons for Defense and Justice organized armed self-defense patrols that protected CORE workers and Black communities across Louisiana, Mississippi, and Alabama, expanding to some 21 chapters by the late 1960s.

claims.object: July 1964 in Jonesboro, Louisiana, after roughly 50 carloads of hooded Klansmen paraded through a Black neighborhood, prompting an informal protective patrol to become a formal defense organization

entities.historicalContext: Composed largely of Black military veterans experienced with firearms, the Deacons operated alongside — and sometimes in tension with — nonviolent civil rights organizations, providing armed protection during CORE campaigns and the 1966 Meredith March Against Fear.

entities.displayName: Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party Challenge at the 1964 Democratic National Convention
entities.nameLower: mississippi freedom democratic party challenge at the 1964 democratic national convention
entities.summary: Sixty-eight Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party delegates, elected through a parallel state convention, traveled to the August 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City to challenge the seating of Mississippi's all-white 'regular' delegation, rejecting a proposed two-seat compromise as insufficient.

claims.object: the seating of Mississippi's all-white 'regular' Democratic delegation at the August 1964 convention, arguing it had been elected through a segregated process that excluded Black voters

entities.historicalContext: Although the MFDP left Atlantic City without official seats, the challenge exposed the exclusion of Black voters from Southern Democratic politics on national television and drove the party's 1968 rule change barring segregated state delegations from future conventions.

entities.summary: For 381 days beginning December 5, 1955, Black Montgomerians organized carpools and walked to work rather than ride segregated city buses. Sparked by Rosa Parks' December 1 arrest and coordinated by the Montgomery Improvement Association under Martin Luther King Jr., the boycott ended December 20, 1956, after Browder v. Gayle desegregated the buses.

claims.object: December 5, 1955, four days after Rosa Parks was arrested on December 1, 1955 for refusing to surrender her bus seat
claims.citationLabel: King Institute Encyclopedia: Montgomery Bus Boycott

claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: The Montgomery Bus Boycott

began on: December 5, 1955, four days after Rosa Parks was arrested on December 1, 1955 for refusing to surrender her bus seat. Cited from kinginstitute.stanford.edu.

entities.historicalContext: The boycott was the first sustained mass protest of the modern civil rights movement. Organized through Black churches, the Women's Political Council, and a carpool system of some 300 vehicles, it demonstrated the economic power of a united Black community and brought Martin Luther King Jr. to national leadership.

entities.summary: On December 1, 1955, Rosa Parks was arrested aboard a Cleveland Avenue bus near the Empire Theater on Montgomery Street after refusing to give up her seat. Troy University's Rosa Parks Museum, opened in 2000 at 252 Montgomery Street, and a 2015 state historical marker commemorate the site where the boycott began.

claims.object: Rosa Parks' December 1, 1955 arrest for refusing to surrender her seat on a segregated Montgomery city bus
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Places of Rosa Parks

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

commemorated by: the Rosa Parks Museum, built on the arrest site at 252 Montgomery Street and opened by Troy University on December 1, 2000. Cited from troy.edu.

entities.historicalContext: Parks, a seamstress and longtime secretary of the Montgomery NAACP, was a trained activist, not an accidental symbol. Her arrest at this downtown stop gave Montgomery's organizers the case they needed, and the museum on the site preserves a restored bus and court records from the boycott that followed.

entities.summary: On March 7, 1965, about 600 voting-rights marchers led by John Lewis and Hosea Williams crossed this U.S. Route 80 bridge over the Alabama River and were attacked by state troopers at its foot — Bloody Sunday. The bridge anchors the Selma to Montgomery National Historic Trail and hosts an annual commemorative crossing.

claims.object: the March 7, 1965 'Bloody Sunday' attack on voting-rights marchers, which triggered national outrage and federal intervention
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Selma to Montgomery National Historic Trail

claims.object: the 54-mile Selma to Montgomery National Historic Trail, designated by Congress in 1996 along the 1965 march route

entities.historicalContext: Named for a Confederate general and Reconstruction-era Klan figure, the 1940 bridge became the visual shorthand for the voting-rights struggle. Marchers crossed it three times in March 1965; the completed march and the images from Bloody Sunday helped push the Voting Rights Act through Congress that August.

entities.summary: Three marches in March 1965 pressed for Black voting rights in Alabama. After Bloody Sunday on March 7 and a turnaround march on March 9, roughly 3,200 marchers left Selma on March 21 under federal protection and arrived at the Montgomery capitol on March 25, 25,000 strong. The Voting Rights Act was signed on August 6, 1965.

claims.object: March 25, 1965, when marchers reached the Alabama capitol in Montgomery after a five-day, 54-mile federally protected march from Selma
claims.citationLabel: King Institute Encyclopedia: Selma to Montgomery March

claims.object: passage of the Voting Rights Act, signed into law on August 6, 1965

contributed to: passage of the Voting Rights Act, signed into law on August 6, 1965. Cited from nps.gov.

entities.historicalContext: The marches grew out of months of voter-registration organizing in Selma and Marion by local activists, SNCC, and SCLC, and out of grief over the police killing of Jimmie Lee Jackson. Their disciplined nonviolence under attack made the case for federal voting-rights legislation to a national audience.

entities.displayName: Little Rock Central High School National Historic Site
entities.nameLower: little rock central high school national historic site
entities.summary: Nine Black students desegregated Little Rock Central High School on September 25, 1957, entering under escort of the 101st Airborne after Governor Orval Faubus used the National Guard to block them. The school, still in operation, was designated a National Historic Site in 1998.

claims.object: the 1957 desegregation crisis in which the Little Rock Nine entered under federal troop escort on September 25, 1957

entities.historicalContext: Three years after Brown v. Board of Education, the Little Rock Nine tested whether the ruling would be enforced. Daisy Bates of the Arkansas NAACP mentored the students through a year of daily harassment; Ernest Green became the school's first Black graduate in May 1958.

entities.displayName: F.W. Woolworth Sit-In Site (International Civil Rights Center & Museum)
entities.nameLower: f.w. woolworth sit-in site (international civil rights center & museum)
entities.summary: On February 1, 1960, four North Carolina A&T freshmen sat at the whites-only lunch counter of the F.W. Woolworth store on South Elm Street and asked to be served. The sit-in grew to hundreds within days, and the counter was desegregated on July 25, 1960. The International Civil Rights Center & Museum opened in the building in 2010.

entities.historicalContext: The Greensboro Four planned their protest in dorm-room conversations at North Carolina A&T. Their disciplined daily return to the counter turned a local action into a regional student movement, and a preserved section of the original lunch counter remains in the museum on the site.

entities.displayName: March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom
entities.nameLower: march on washington for jobs and freedom
entities.summary: The March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom drew an estimated 250,000 people to the Lincoln Memorial on August 28, 1963. Organized by A. Philip Randolph and Bayard Rustin with a coalition of civil rights, labor, and faith groups, it pressed for civil rights legislation and closed with Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I Have a Dream' speech.

entities.historicalContext: Rustin's team assembled the logistics for the largest demonstration the capital had seen in under two months — trains, buses, marshals, and a printed manual. The march linked economic demands to civil rights and is credited with building momentum for the Civil Rights Act of 1964.

entities.summary: Kelly Ingram Park, across from Sixteenth Street Baptist Church, was the assembly ground of the 1963 Birmingham campaign, where young demonstrators of the May 1963 Children's Crusade were met with police dogs and fire hoses. Their discipline under attack helped move the Civil Rights Act of 1964. The park lies within Birmingham Civil Rights National Monument, designated in 2017.

entities.historicalContext: Project C, planned by Fred Shuttlesworth's Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights and the SCLC, filled Birmingham's jails with volunteers in spring 1963. Thousands of students marched from the church through this park; national coverage of the city's response pushed the Kennedy administration to propose civil rights legislation.

entities.summary: The Lorraine Motel on Mulberry Street, a Green Book-era lodging for Black travelers, is where Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated on April 4, 1968, while in Memphis supporting the sanitation workers' strike. The National Civil Rights Museum opened at the site in 1991, preserving Room 306 and the motel facade.

claims.object: the assassination of Martin Luther King Jr. on the motel balcony outside Room 306 on April 4, 1968

entities.historicalContext: King came to Memphis to stand with 1,300 striking sanitation workers whose 'I AM A MAN' campaign joined labor rights to civil rights. The motel where he stayed on Southern trips was saved from demolition by a community foundation and rebuilt as a museum anchored on the preserved room where he spent his last night.

entities.displayName: Medgar and Myrlie Evers Home National Monument
entities.nameLower: medgar and myrlie evers home national monument
entities.summary: The ranch house at 2332 Margaret Walker Alexander Drive was home to Mississippi NAACP field secretary Medgar Evers, who organized voter registration and boycotts statewide until his assassination in the driveway on June 12, 1963. The home was designated a National Historic Landmark in 2017 and a national monument in 2020.

entities.historicalContext: Evers investigated the Emmett Till lynching, organized the Jackson boycott movement, and built NAACP chapters across Mississippi under constant threat. Myrlie Evers-Williams preserved the house and pursued the case for three decades until Byron De La Beckwith's 1994 conviction; the family donated the home to Tougaloo College in 1993.

entities.summary: At Montgomery's former Greyhound station on South Court Street, Freedom Riders testing interstate bus desegregation were attacked by a mob on May 20, 1961. The riders pressed on, and by November 1961 the Interstate Commerce Commission's desegregation order took effect. The Alabama Historical Commission opened the Freedom Rides Museum in the station in 2011.

entities.historicalContext: CORE and Nashville student movement riders kept the Freedom Rides going after earlier violence in Anniston and Birmingham. The night after the station attack, a mass meeting at First Baptist Church stood besieged until federalized troops arrived — a confrontation that forced the Kennedy administration into the movement's corner.

entities.displayName: Robert Russa Moton High School (Moton Museum)
entities.nameLower: robert russa moton high school (moton museum)
entities.summary: On April 23, 1951, sixteen-year-old Barbara Johns led some 450 classmates out of overcrowded Robert Russa Moton High School to demand a school equal to Farmville's white high school. Their strike produced Davis v. County School Board, one of the five cases decided in Brown v. Board of Education. The building is now the Moton Museum.

claims.object: the April 23, 1951 student strike led by Barbara Johns against unequal school facilities in Prince Edward County

entities.historicalContext: Built for 200 students but holding 450 in tar-paper annexes, Moton embodied the inequality of 'separate but equal.' After Brown, Prince Edward County closed its entire public school system from 1959 to 1964 rather than integrate. The school was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1998 and affiliated with Brown v. Board NHP in 2022.

entities.summary: From July 19 to August 11, 1958, Wichita NAACP Youth Council members led by Ron Walters and Carol Parks sat in daily at the downtown Dockum Drug Store lunch counter at Douglas and Broadway. The chain agreed to serve all customers statewide — a successful student-led sit-in a year and a half before Greensboro.

entities.historicalContext: The Dockum sit-in is among the earliest sustained, successful student sit-ins of the movement, predating Greensboro by eighteen months. Chester I. Lewis Reflection Square Park, with a bronze lunch-counter sculpture, now marks the corner; Ron Walters went on to become a leading political scientist.

entities.summary: On August 27, 1956, twelve Black students walked down Foley Hill into Clinton High School, making it the first state-supported public high school in the former Confederacy to desegregate. They kept attending through mob harassment and a 1958 bombing of the school; Bobby Cain graduated in May 1957. The Green McAdoo Cultural Center tells their story.

notabilityBasis.note: began on: August 27, 1956, when twelve Black students entered Clinton High School under a federal court desegregation order. Cited from greenmcadooculturalcenter.org.
commemorated by: the Green McAdoo Cultural Center, opened in 2006 in the former segregated Green McAdoo Grammar School with bronze statues of the twelve students. Cited from tnmuseum.org.

entities.historicalContext: Clinton desegregated a year before Little Rock, after the McSwain v. Anderson County ruling. When outside agitators stirred mobs, white and Black neighbors escorted students and the National Guard restored order; Bobby Cain became the first Black graduate of an integrated state-supported high school in the South.

entities.summary: Founded by the American Missionary Association after the Civil War to educate freedpeople, Dorchester Academy's surviving 1934 boys' dormitory housed the SCLC's Citizenship Education Program from 1961 to 1970. There Septima Clark and Dorothy Cotton trained community teachers who prepared thousands of Black Southerners to register to vote.

claims.object: the SCLC Citizenship Education Program, 1961-1970, which trained close to 2,000 teachers who in turn taught more than 10,000 others
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Dorchester Academy Boys' Dormitory

claims.object: a National Historic Landmark in 2006; the dormitory is the last surviving building of the academy campus

notabilityBasis.note: headquarters of: the SCLC Citizenship Education Program, 1961-1970, which trained close to 2,000 teachers who in turn taught more than 10,000 others. Cited from nps.gov.
designated: a National Historic Landmark in 2006; the dormitory is the last surviving building of the academy campus. Cited from civilrightstrail.com.

entities.historicalContext: The citizenship schools begun by Septima Clark taught literacy, constitutional rights, and the mechanics of registration, quietly building the voter base the movement's marches depended on. SCLC leadership also used Dorchester for retreats, including planning sessions ahead of the 1963 Birmingham campaign.

entities.summary: Highlander Folk School near Monteagle trained labor and civil rights organizers across lines of race from 1932 until Tennessee revoked its charter in 1961. Rosa Parks attended a two-week desegregation workshop led by Septima Clark in August 1955, four months before her Montgomery arrest; the citizenship school model begun here spread across the South.

claims.object: Rosa Parks at an August 1955 desegregation workshop led by Septima Clark, months before the Montgomery Bus Boycott
claims.citationLabel: Library of Congress: Rosa Parks and Highlander Folk School

claims.object: 1961, when Tennessee revoked its charter and confiscated the property; the school rechartered as the Highlander Research and Education Center

entities.locationLabel: Former Highlander Folk School campus, Summerfield community near Monteagle
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: trained: Rosa Parks at an August 1955 desegregation workshop led by Septima Clark, months before the Montgomery Bus Boycott. Cited from loc.gov.
closed in: 1961, when Tennessee revoked its charter and confiscated the property; the school rechartered as the Highlander Research and Education Center. Cited from kinginstitute.stanford.edu.

entities.historicalContext: Founded by Myles Horton in 1932, Highlander was one of the few Southern places where Black and white organizers met as equals. Septima Clark's citizenship school curriculum moved from here to the SCLC, and 'We Shall Overcome' entered the movement through Highlander's music workshops.

entities.displayName: Mason Temple, Church of God in Christ
entities.nameLower: mason temple, church of god in christ
entities.summary: Mason Temple, world headquarters of the Church of God in Christ and one of the largest Black-owned assembly halls in the country when completed in 1945, hosted mass meetings of the 1968 Memphis sanitation workers' strike. Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his final speech, 'I've Been to the Mountaintop,' here on April 3, 1968.

claims.object: Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I've Been to the Mountaintop' speech on April 3, 1968, the night before his assassination
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: Mason Temple, Memphis

claims.object: between 1940 and 1945 as the administrative and spiritual center of the Church of God in Christ

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: site of: Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'I've Been to the Mountaintop' speech on April 3, 1968, the night before his assassination. Cited from nps.gov.
built: between 1940 and 1945 as the administrative and spiritual center of the Church of God in Christ. Cited from memphisheritage.org.

entities.historicalContext: Named for COGIC founder Bishop Charles Harrison Mason, the temple anchored Black religious life in Memphis and opened its hall to the sanitation workers' movement in 1968. King spoke there to some 3,000 people through a storm, closing with words that became his valediction.

entities.summary: Bethel Baptist Church in Collegeville served as headquarters of the Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights from 1956 to 1961 under pastor Fred Shuttlesworth, sustaining Birmingham's desegregation campaign after Alabama outlawed the NAACP. Bombed three times — 1956, 1958, and 1962 — the congregation rebuilt each time. It became a National Historic Landmark in 2005.

claims.object: the Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights, 1956-1961, led by pastor Fred Shuttlesworth
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: The Historic Bethel Baptist Church

claims.object: bombings on December 25, 1956, June 29, 1958, and December 14, 1962, continuing weekly mass meetings throughout

entities.locationLabel: Historic Bethel Baptist Church, 3233 29th Avenue North, Collegeville, Birmingham
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: headquarters of: the Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights, 1956-1961, led by pastor Fred Shuttlesworth. Cited from nps.gov.
survived: bombings on December 25, 1956, June 29, 1958, and December 14, 1962, continuing weekly mass meetings throughout. Cited from thehistoricbethel.org.

entities.historicalContext: When Alabama banned the NAACP in 1956, Shuttlesworth founded the ACMHR at Bethel the same week, and the church became the launch point for challenges to bus segregation and school exclusion in Birmingham. Its partnership with the SCLC brought the 1963 Birmingham campaign that helped produce the Civil Rights Act of 1964.

entities.summary: Ellen F. Eglin (died c. 1915), a Washington, D.C. housekeeper and later Census Office clerk, invented a clothes wringer in the 1880s. In 1888 she sold the rights for $18 rather than patent in her own name, telling The Woman Inventor she feared white customers would refuse a wringer known to come from a Black woman.

claims.object: a clothes wringer whose commercial rights she sold to an agent in 1888 for $18, explaining to Charlotte Smith of The Woman Inventor that she feared market rejection if buyers knew a Black woman had patented it
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast: Ellen Eglin (1849–?)

claims.object: a charwoman in the U.S. Census Office under the Department of the Interior by 1890, while living with her brother Charles at 1929 11th Street NW in Washington, D.C.

entities.historicalContext: Eglin's sale of her wringer is a documented case of how racial barriers shaped who could claim credit and profit from late-19th-century domestic inventions.

entities.summary: Marjorie Stewart Joyner (1896–1994) died in Chicago's Bronzeville after a career as national adviser to Madam C.J. Walker's beauty schools. She patented a permanent-wave machine in 1928 and later helped write Illinois cosmetology law and co-founded national Black beautician associations with Mary McLeod Bethune.

claims.object: U.S. Patent 1,693,515 (November 27, 1928) for a permanent-wave machine that used rods on a table apparatus with a scalp protector so curls or straight styles lasted several days
claims.citationLabel: MIT Lemelson Center: Marjorie Joyner

entities.historicalContext: Joyner's machine entered Black and white salon practice even though Walker's company held the patent credit; her later organizing with Bethune built professional infrastructure for African American beauticians.

entities.summary: Christine Darden (b. 1942) joined NASA Langley in 1967 as a human computer, earned a doctorate in engineering, and led sonic-boom research for high-speed flight. She became the first African American woman at Langley promoted into the Senior Executive Service and received the Congressional Gold Medal in 2019.

claims.object: NASA Langley's sonic boom group within the High Speed Research program, designing wing and nose configurations to reduce sonic-boom noise for proposed high-speed civil transport
claims.citationLabel: NASA Langley Hall of Honor: Christine M. Darden

claims.object: the Senior Executive Service as the first African American woman at NASA Langley to reach that federal civil-service rank, later directing strategic communication and education before retiring in 2007

entities.historicalContext: Darden's path from the Langley computer pool to SES leadership continues the West Area Computing lineage associated with Katherine Johnson, Dorothy Vaughan, and Mary Jackson, and she is one of the researchers profiled in Margot Lee Shetterly's Hidden Figures.

entities.summary: Carter G. Woodson (1875–1950) died in 1950 after founding the Association for the Study of Negro Life and History and launching Negro History Week in 1926 — the seed of Black History Month. From his Washington office-home he edited the Journal of Negro History under Jim Crow.

entities.nameLower: boston african american national historic site
entities.summary: Boston African American National Historic Site preserves and interprets Beacon Hill's 19th-century free Black community, the Black Heritage Trail, Underground Railroad networks, and the Massachusetts 54th Infantry Regiment story. Ranger-led and self-guided tours connect meeting houses, residences, and schools that anchored abolitionist Boston.

entities.historicalContext: Beacon Hill's Black community built churches, schools, and mutual-aid institutions that made Boston a northern hub of abolition and enlistment for the 54th Massachusetts.

entities.summary: Designed by J. Edward Storck and opened in 1910 at 620 T Street NW, the Howard Theatre served Black audiences and performers for more than five decades as one of the nation's oldest theaters built expressly for African American entertainment — a peer of the Apollo, the Royal in Baltimore, and Philadelphia's Pearl.

entities.historicalContext: Segregated booking circuits made it difficult for Black artists to develop and receive recognition; the Howard, along with theaters in Harlem, Baltimore, and Philadelphia, provided the stages on which many of the most prominent entertainers of the mid-twentieth century debuted before desegregation and urban change reshaped Washington's U Street corridor.

entities.summary: Built in 1912 as the Star Theater at 1615 East 18th Street and renamed the Gem after a 1924 terra cotta renovation, this Kansas City movie and vaudeville house anchored the 18th and Vine district as one of the best-known theaters serving the city's Black community through the jazz age.

entities.historicalContext: The Gem stood alongside the New Rialto and Eblon theaters on 18th and Vine Streets as Kansas City's Black commercial and nightlife center incubated the swing-era bands that made the district nationally synonymous with jazz.

entities.summary: Charles Henry Douglass, Macon's leading Black entrepreneur, opened the present Douglass Theatre in 1921 at 355 Martin Luther King Jr. Boulevard after earlier venues dating to 1912. Affiliated with the Theatre Owners Booking Association, it hosted Bessie Smith, Ma Rainey, Duke Ellington, and Otis Redding before closing in 1973 and reopening after restoration in 1997.

claims.object: The New Douglass Theatre opened in 1921 at 355–359 Broadway (now Martin Luther King Jr. Boulevard) with 750–800 seats in a facility designed primarily for motion pictures while continuing live vauvelville and blues performances
claims.citationLabel: Introduction to the Douglass Theatre in Macon

entities.historicalContext: The Douglass Theatre's surviving business records document Macon's place on the Chitlin Circuit and the programming choices — race films, TOBA vaudeville, and local talent shows — that shaped Black leisure in central Georgia before integration.

entities.summary: Built in 1924 and opened in 1925 at 821 Eighth Avenue, the Liberty Theatre was Columbus, Georgia's first theater built solely for Black patrons and one of the largest movie houses in the city. Columbus native Ma Rainey and other blues and jazz artists performed on its stage until it closed in 1974; it reopened in 1996 as a performing arts cultural center.

claims.object: The Liberty Theatre opened in late April 1925 at 821 Eighth Avenue as Columbus's first black theater, built by the Martin Theater chain with seating for about 600 and a balcony, stage, and projection room

entities.historicalContext: Few Southern cities had a theater built exclusively for Black patrons in the 1920s; the Liberty's scale and Martin chain ownership made it a regional anchor for race films, stage shows, and the blues traditions linked to Ma Rainey's Columbus roots.

entities.summary: Opened in 1935 at 1631 Fourth Avenue North in Birmingham's Fourth Avenue Historic District, the Carver Theatre was a segregated-era movie palace where African Americans could see first-run films. Restored by the city as the Carver Performing Arts Center, it now houses the Alabama Jazz Hall of Fame.

entities.summary: Opening in 1921 as the Douglass Theatre and reopening by 1925 as the Royal, the Pennsylvania Avenue movie and vaudeville palace ranked with the Apollo, Howard, Regal, and Earl theaters as a premier stage for Black entertainment. Nat King Cole, Billie Holiday, Duke Ellington, and later Motown acts played the Royal until it closed in 1971 and was demolished.

notabilityBasis.note: opened: Architect Jefferson Powell designed the Art Deco Ritz Theatre, completed in September 1929 at 829 North Davis Street as one of LaVilla's primary motion picture venues for African American audiences. Cited from The Jaxson.

reopened as: After preservation efforts secured River City Renaissance funding, the Ritz Theatre and Museum opened on September 30, 1999, incorporating the original building's northwest corner marquee with a new theater and 11,000-square-foot museum. Cited from Ritz Theatre and Museum.

entities.historicalContext: LaVilla's railroad-era Black professional class supported churches, fraternal halls, and theaters along Davis Street; the Ritz's revival documents Northeast Florida's Gullah Geechee urban heritage after decades of highway construction and disinvestment.

entities.summary: Opened February 4, 1928 at 4719 South Parkway (now Martin Luther King Jr. Drive) in Bronzeville, the Regal Theater was among the first U.S. entertainment complexes built expressly for Black audiences, seating about 3,000 and employing Black management and staff while hosting Duke Ellington, Ella Fitzgerald, Nat King Cole, and the Jackson Five until closing about 1968 and demolition in 1973.

claims.object: The National Theater Corporation built the Regal Theater at 4719–27 South Parkway for $1.5 million; it opened February 4, 1928 as one of the first theaters in the country built expressly to cater to the Black community
claims.citationSource: City of Chicago — Landmark Designation Report
claims.citationLabel: New Regal Theater landmark designation report (original Regal history)

claims.object: The Regal presented films, dance, comedy, and jazz, booking Ray Charles, Ella Fitzgerald, Miles Davis, Nat King Cole, Duke Ellington, and B.B. King for Bronzeville audiences barred from most Loop palaces
claims.citationLabel: Regal Theater, Chicago

entities.locationLabel: 4719 South Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Drive, Bronzeville (former Regal Theater site)

entities.historicalContext: The Regal and adjacent Savoy Ballroom made 47th and South Parkway Chicago's answer to Harlem; community protest kept the Regal open when Balaban & Katz attempted to close it during the Depression, preserving a flagship stage for the Great Migration's South Side cultural capital.

entities.summary: Designed by A. Hays Town and opened August 25, 1950 at 1305 Myrtle Walk, the Lincoln Theatre was Baton Rouge's primary movie and entertainment venue for African Americans during segregation, seating about 600 until closing in 1986. Listed in the National Register in 2010, the building is undergoing restoration as a cultural center.

claims.object: The Lincoln Theater opened on August 25, 1950 at the corner of Myrtle Walk and Eddie Robinson Street as an important entertainment venue for Baton Rouge's large African American population during the segregation era

entities.historicalContext: Mid-century Baton Rouge's Black neighborhoods supported a parallel commercial life along Myrtle Walk; the Lincoln's Art Moderne design and downtown proximity made it one of the few places where African American families could see first-run films together under Jim Crow.

entities.summary: Built in 1923 at 1103 Texas Avenue by the Grand Court Order of Calanthe under Cora M. Allen, this four-story Classical Revival temple housed Black professional offices and a rooftop ballroom where Louis Armstrong, Duke Ellington, Count Basie, and Cab Calloway performed for Shreveport audiences barred from downtown white clubs.

claims.object: The four-story Classical Revival Calanthean Temple was built in 1923, housing Black professional offices, the Calanthean women's organization, and a rooftop ballroom that became a major social venue
claims.citationSource: National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places
claims.citationLabel: St. Paul's Bottoms Historic District — Calanthean Temple

claims.object: The rooftop ballroom hosted packed audiences for Louis Armstrong, Cab Calloway, Duke Ellington, Count Basie, and other jazz greats traveling between northern tours and New Orleans

entities.historicalContext: Texas Avenue's Antioch Baptist Church, Palace Ballroom, and Calanthean Temple formed Shreveport's Black commercial spine; the temple's scale — reported as the tallest building in the city when built — signaled the economic reach of Calantheans and Black professionals under segregation.

entities.summary: Remodeled from a 1909 automobile garage in 1921, the Sunset Cafe at 315 East 35th Street became one of Chicago's earliest integrated Black-and-tan jazz clubs. Louis Armstrong, Earl Hines, Johnny Dodds, and Benny Goodman played its house orchestra before a 1937 remodeling renamed it the Grand Terrace Cafe, which remained popular until 1950.

claims.object: Following a 1921 remodeling, the Sunset Cafe became a legendary jazz venue; the building was designated a Chicago Landmark on September 9, 1998 as part of the Black Metropolis-Bronzeville Historic District
claims.citationLabel: Sunset Cafe — Chicago Landmark

claims.object: After a 1937 remodeling the venue became the Grand Terrace Cafe and remained a popular nightclub until 1950; original Jazz Age murals survive on interior walls
claims.citationSource: Commission on Chicago Landmarks — historical marker
claims.citationLabel: Sunset Cafe historical marker

entities.locationLabel: 315 East 35th Street, Bronzeville
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

entities.historicalContext: Unlike New York's demolished Cotton Club site, the Sunset/Grand Terrace building still stands in Bronzeville with period murals — a rare physical trace of the integrated jazz incubators that made Chicago a creative capital between 1917 and the bebop era.

entities.summary: From Christmas Eve 1941 through 1951, Orchestra Hall at 3711 Woodward Avenue operated as the Paradise Theater, Detroit's premier jazz venue in the Paradise Valley neighborhood adjacent to Black Bottom. Ella Fitzgerald, Billie Holiday, Duke Ellington, Louis Armstrong, and Cab Calloway played the hall before it returned to symphonic use as Orchestra Hall.

claims.object: Orchestra Hall presented jazz artists under the name Paradise Theater from December 24, 1941 until 1951, hosting Ella Fitzgerald, Billie Holiday, Count Basie, Duke Ellington, and Cab Calloway for Black Bottom and Paradise Valley audiences
claims.citationLabel: Paradise Valley — Detroit Historical Society

claims.object: The building, designed by C. Howard Crane and opened in 1919 as Orchestra Hall, was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1971; the Library of Congress documents its later use as the Paradise Theatre

claims.citationLabel: Orchestra Hall / Paradise Theatre, Detroit

Howard Crane and opened in 1919 as Orchestra Hall, was listed in the National Register of Historic Places in 1971; the Library of Congress documents its later use as the Paradise Theatre. Cited from Library of Congress — Historic American Buildings Survey.

entities.historicalContext: Paradise Valley's Flame Show Bar, Club Harlem, and Paradise Theater channeled Great Migration wages into a dense Black entertainment economy until postwar urban renewal and the 1943 race riot reduced mixed audiences; the hall's 1970s preservation campaign saved one of Crane's finest acoustical designs.

entities.summary: Opening Thanksgiving Eve 1927 at 4733 South Parkway in Bronzeville, the Savoy Ballroom seated roughly 3,800 dancers in a refined setting that showcased Louis Armstrong, Count Basie, and other national bands seven nights a week until closing in 1948. Built alongside the Regal Theater, it was demolished in the 1970s; the Harold Washington Cultural Center now occupies the site.

claims.object: The Savoy Ballroom opened on Thanksgiving Eve, November 23, 1927, at 4733 South Parkway as part of a $1 million commercial development that also included the Regal Theater and South Center department store
claims.citationLabel: Savoy Ballroom — Jazz Age Chicago

claims.object: Two major jazz bands alternated nightly at the Savoy, which accommodated approximately 3,800 dancers and featured Louis Armstrong, Count Basie, and other leading bands until it closed in 1948
claims.citationLabel: Stroll Through 7 Sites of Jazz History in Chicago

entities.locationLabel: 4733 South Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. Drive, Bronzeville (former Savoy Ballroom site)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: opened: The Savoy Ballroom opened on Thanksgiving Eve, November 23, 1927, at 4733 South Parkway as part of a $1 million commercial development that also included the Regal Theater and South Center department store. Cited from Jazz Age Chicago.

hosted performances: Two major jazz bands alternated nightly at the Savoy, which accommodated approximately 3,800 dancers and featured Louis Armstrong, Count Basie, and other leading bands until it closed in 1948. Cited from National Trust for Historic Preservation.

entities.historicalContext: Promoter I. Jay Faggen modeled Chicago's Savoy on Harlem's namesake ballroom; the South Side Chamber of Commerce, Binga State Bank, and the Chicago Defender held events there, tying Bronzeville's business elite to the dance-floor modernity of the jazz age.

entities.summary: Completed in 1921 at 1901–1903 Seventh Street NW, the Southern Aid Society–Dunbar Theater Building combined the Washington headquarters of the nation's oldest Black life insurance company with the Dunbar Theatre movie house designed by Reginald W. Geare on the first floor — a mixed-use anchor of the Seventh and U Street Black commercial corridor.

claims.object: Black architect Isaiah T. Hatton designed the four-story building completed in 1921; Reginald W. Geare designed the Dunbar Theatre on the first floor for the Murray Brothers, with offices and apartments above for Black tenants
claims.citationSource: National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places

entities.locationLabel: 1901–1903 Seventh Street Northwest, Shaw

Cited from National Park Service — National Register of Historic Places.

entities.historicalContext: Facing the Howard Theatre across T Street, the Dunbar gave segregated Washington a Black-owned cinema and insurance headquarters in one building — part of a U Street economy that also included the Whitelaw Hotel, True Reformer Building, and Murray Palace Casino.

entities.summary: By 1921 Greenwood Avenue anchored roughly 35 square blocks of Black-owned groceries, hotels, theaters, newspapers, and law and medical offices — the 'Black Wall Street' of the nation. Residents rebuilt after the district's 1921 destruction, and by the 1940s Greenwood again supported more than 200 Black-owned businesses.

claims.object: Black Wall Street — a nationally renowned, self-contained Black entrepreneurial district along Greenwood Avenue with nightclubs, hotels, cafes, newspapers, clothiers, theaters, and professional offices
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia of Oklahoma History and Culture: Greenwood District

claims.object: Tulsa Race Massacre of May 31 – June 1, 1921, which left the Greenwood area in ruins; residents subsequently rebuilt the district
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: John Hope Franklin Reconciliation Park

entities.locationLabel: Greenwood District, Tulsa
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.
