entities.historicalContext: In 1945, Adams commanded the 6888th Central Postal Directory Battalion, the only African American and multi-ethnic WAC unit deployed overseas, which cleared a six-month mail backlog in England in three months; she and the battalion received the Congressional Gold Medal in 2022.

entities.summary: Nancy Leftenant-Colon, repeatedly denied entry to the military as a Black nurse, was accepted as an Army Nurse Corps reservist in 1945 and, after President Truman's 1948 desegregation order, became the first Black woman integrated into the Regular Army Nurse Corps.

claims.object: Following President Truman's Executive Order 9981 desegregating the military, Leftenant-Colon applied for and received regular Army Nurse Corps status in 1948, becoming the first Black woman integrated into the Regular Army Nurse Corps.
claims.citationLabel: Army Times: Nancy Leftenant-Colon, First Black Woman in Army Nurse Corps, Has Died

claims.object: After graduating from nursing school in 1941 and being repeatedly rejected because of her race, Leftenant-Colon was accepted as an Army Nurse Corps reservist in January 1945.
claims.citationLabel: The Washington Post: Nancy Leftenant-Colon, Barrier-Breaking Military Nurse, Dies at 104

entities.locationLabel: Amityville, New York, Leftenant-Colon's longtime home
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: served as: After graduating from nursing school in 1941 and being repeatedly rejected because of her race, Leftenant-Colon was accepted as an Army Nurse Corps reservist in January 1945.. Cited from washingtonpost.com.

first to: Following President Truman's Executive Order 9981 desegregating the military, Leftenant-Colon applied for and received regular Army Nurse Corps status in 1948, becoming the first Black woman integrated into the Regular Army Nurse Corps.. Cited from armytimes.com.

entities.historicalContext: Leftenant-Colon later served as a flight nurse in the Air Force and died in Amityville, New York, in January 2025 at age 104.

entities.displayName: Frederick Douglass (U.S. Marshal and 1872 Vice-Presidential Nominee)
entities.nameLower: frederick douglass (u.s. marshal and 1872 vice-presidential nominee)
entities.summary: Beyond his abolitionist career, Frederick Douglass was confirmed by the Senate on March 17, 1877, as U.S. Marshal for the District of Columbia, the first African American to hold the post, and in 1872 became the first Black person nominated for U.S. Vice President, on Victoria Woodhull's Equal Rights Party ticket.

claims.object: The Senate confirmed Douglass as U.S. Marshal for the District of Columbia on March 17, 1877, making him the first African American to hold the post.
claims.citationLabel: Boundary Stones (WETA): Frederick Douglass's Career in D.C. Government

claims.object: In 1872, Douglass became the first African American nominated for Vice President of the United States, named without his knowledge as Victoria Woodhull's running mate on the Equal Rights Party ticket.
claims.citationLabel: The Harvard Gazette: 1872 Election, Victoria Woodhull Picks Frederick Douglass as VP

entities.locationLabel: Cedar Hill (Frederick Douglass National Historic Site), 1411 W Street SE, Washington, D.C.
notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: sworn in on: The Senate confirmed Douglass as U.S. Marshal for the District of Columbia on March 17, 1877, making him the first African American to hold the post.. Cited from boundarystones.weta.org.

first to: In 1872, Douglass became the first African American nominated for Vice President of the United States, named without his knowledge as Victoria Woodhull's running mate on the Equal Rights Party ticket.. Cited from news.harvard.edu.

entities.historicalContext: This record is anchored at Cedar Hill, Douglass's Anacostia home from 1877 until his death in 1895, as his primary documented Washington residence during the years of both these government-related firsts; the site itself is catalogued separately as an existing place-kind entry.

entities.summary: Edward R. Dudley, a civil rights lawyer recruited by Thurgood Marshall to the NAACP, was promoted to Ambassador upon the 1949 elevation of the U.S. mission in Liberia, becoming the first African American to hold the rank of U.S. Ambassador.

claims.object: In 1949, when the U.S. mission in Liberia was elevated to a full embassy, Dudley was promoted to Ambassador, becoming the first African American to hold the rank of U.S. Ambassador.
claims.citationLabel: National Museum of American Diplomacy: The Legacy of Edward R. Dudley, First African American Ambassador

claims.object: As ambassador, Dudley documented and challenged racial discrimination against Black State Department employees, prompting a change in assignment practices after meeting with Undersecretary of State Dean Acheson.
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopaedia Britannica: Edward R. Dudley

entities.locationLabel: Roanoke, Virginia, Dudley's hometown
notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: first to: In 1949, when the U.S. mission in Liberia was elevated to a full embassy, Dudley was promoted to Ambassador, becoming the first African American to hold the rank of U.S. Ambassador.. Cited from diplomacy.state.gov; britannica.com.

entities.historicalContext: Dudley later served as a justice of the New York State Supreme Court; his diplomatic career is commemorated in his hometown of Roanoke, Virginia.

entities.summary: James Wormley Jones, a Washington, D.C. police detective and decorated World War I infantry captain, was sworn in on November 19, 1919, as the first African American special agent of the Bureau of Investigation, later the FBI.

claims.object: Jones was appointed the first African American special agent of the Bureau of Investigation on November 19, 1919, by director A. Bruce Bielaski, assigned to its General Intelligence Division.
claims.citationLabel: Federal Bureau of Investigation: Our History, Our Service, 100 Years of African American Special Agents

claims.object: As an undercover agent known internally as Agent 800, Jones's investigations included the case against Marcus Garvey and the African Blood Brotherhood before he left the Bureau in 1923.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: James Wormley Jones (1884-1958)

entities.locationLabel: Washington, D.C., where Jones served in the Bureau of Investigation's General Intelligence Division
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

entities.historicalContext: Before joining the Bureau, Jones served with the Washington Metropolitan Police Department and commanded a company of the 368th Infantry, 92nd Division, in combat in France during World War I.

entities.summary: Patrick H. Raymond, son of a formerly enslaved abolitionist, was appointed Chief Engineer of the Cambridge, Massachusetts Fire Department on January 5, 1871, becoming the first known Black fire chief in the United States. He commanded the department during the Great Boston Fire of 1872.

claims.object: Cambridge Mayor Hamlin Harding appointed Raymond Chief Engineer of the Cambridge Fire Department on January 5, 1871, making him the first known Black fire chief in the United States.
claims.citationLabel: City of Cambridge, Massachusetts: Chief Patrick H. Raymond, First Black Fire Chief in the Nation

claims.object: As chief, Raymond commanded the Cambridge Fire Department's response to the Great Fire of Boston in 1872, working alongside Boston Fire Chief Damrell.
claims.citationLabel: The Cambridge Room (Cambridge Public Library): Patrick Henry Raymond, America's First Black Fire Chief

entities.locationLabel: Cambridge, Massachusetts, where Raymond served as fire chief
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

entities.historicalContext: Raymond's father was a formerly enslaved abolitionist and early pastor of Boston's African Meeting House; Raymond served as chief until 1879 and died in Cambridge in 1892.

entities.summary: Lee P. Brown led police departments in Atlanta, Houston, and New York City before becoming, in 1997, the first African American elected mayor of Houston, Texas, serving three terms through 2004.

claims.object: In 1997, Brown was elected the first African American mayor of Houston, Texas, and was re-elected twice, serving through 2004.
claims.citationLabel: Watch The Yard: Dr. Lee Brown Was Houston's First Black Mayor

claims.object: In 1982, Brown became the first African American appointed police chief of Houston, developing the department's Neighborhood Oriented Policing program during his tenure through 1990.
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Lee P. Brown (1937- )

entities.locationLabel: Houston, Texas, where Brown served as police chief and later mayor
notabilityLabels: The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: elected on: In 1997, Brown was elected the first African American mayor of Houston, Texas, and was re-elected twice, serving through 2004.. Cited from watchtheyard.com.

entities.historicalContext: Brown also served as Atlanta's public safety commissioner from 1978 to 1982 and as New York City police commissioner from 1990 to 1992, before leading the White House drug policy office under President Clinton.

entities.summary: Willie L. Williams became Philadelphia's first African American police commissioner in 1988, then in 1992 the first Black chief of the Los Angeles Police Department, leading it through the department's post-Rodney King reform effort.

claims.object: Williams was appointed Philadelphia's police commissioner in 1988, the first African American to hold the position, serving four years and earning praise for improving police-community relations.
claims.citationLabel: The Philadelphia Inquirer: Willie L. Williams, First African American Top Cop in Both Philadelphia and Los Angeles

claims.object: In 1992, Williams became the first African American chief of the Los Angeles Police Department, taking over after Daryl Gates's resignation following the Rodney King unrest.
claims.citationLabel: National Law Enforcement Officers Memorial Fund: Willie L. Williams

entities.locationLabel: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, where Williams began his law enforcement career

entities.historicalContext: Williams served as LAPD chief until 1997 and later worked as federal security director for Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport.

entities.summary: Established February 25, 1837, through Quaker philanthropist Richard Humphreys' bequest of $10,000 to educate people of African descent and prepare them as teachers. Begun in Philadelphia as the Institute for Colored Youth, it is the nation's oldest historically Black institution of higher learning.

claims.citationLabel: The First HBCU page

claims.object: Oldest historically Black institution of higher education in the United States
claims.citationLabel: The First HBCU page

entities.locationLabel: Cheyney University campus, Delaware and Chester Counties
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the only or oldest surviving example of its kind in a defined scope (oldest Black-owned business in a city, only remaining Rosenwald school in a county) with evidence supporting the superlative claim.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1837. Cited from Cheyney University of Pennsylvania.

entities.historicalContext: Cheyney's founding predates emancipation by nearly three decades, opening the era of institution-building that all later HBCUs would extend. Its early decades in Philadelphia produced generations of Black teachers for schools across the country.

entities.summary: Chartered April 29, 1854, as Ashmun Institute by John Miller Dickey and Sarah Emlen Cresson, becoming the nation's first degree-granting HBCU. Renamed for Abraham Lincoln in 1866, it educated Thurgood Marshall, Langston Hughes, and future heads of state across Africa.

claims.citationLabel: University history page

entities.historicalContext: Founded before the Civil War, Lincoln belongs to the small group of antebellum institutions that proved higher education for Black men could be built and sustained. Its alumni network shaped law, letters, and independence movements on two continents.

entities.summary: Founded in 1856 at Tawawa Springs near Xenia, Ohio, by the Methodist Episcopal Church and purchased in 1863 by the A.M.E. Church under Bishop Daniel A. Payne, making it the nation's first college owned and operated by African Americans. Payne became the first Black president of an American university.

claims.citationLabel: About Wilberforce page

claims.object: First college owned and operated by African Americans, under the A.M.E. Church from 1863
claims.citationLabel: University of Firsts page

entities.locationLabel: Wilberforce University campus, Greene County
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1856. Cited from Wilberforce University.
first in nation: First college owned and operated by African Americans, under the A.M.E. Church from 1863. Cited from Wilberforce University.

entities.historicalContext: Named for abolitionist William Wilberforce, the university demonstrated Black institutional self-governance a decade before Reconstruction. Its A.M.E. stewardship set a model of church-sustained higher education that other denominations followed.

entities.summary: Chartered by Congress on March 2, 1867, and named for Freedmen's Bureau commissioner Gen. Oliver O. Howard. From a single frame building it grew into a comprehensive research university whose law school trained the litigators, Charles Hamilton Houston and Thurgood Marshall among them, who dismantled legal segregation.

claims.citationLabel: University history page

claims.object: School of Law, established 1869, incubated the legal strategy behind Brown v. Board of Education
claims.citationLabel: Law school history page

entities.locationLabel: Main campus, Georgia Avenue NW, Shaw and Pleasant Plains
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1867. Cited from Howard University.
notable program: School of Law, established 1869, incubated the legal strategy behind Brown v. Board of Education. Cited from Howard University School of Law.

entities.historicalContext: Howard anchored the cluster of federally supported institutions created in the capital during Reconstruction. Through the twentieth century its faculty and graduates supplied much of the intellectual leadership of Black America, from medicine to the courtroom.

entities.summary: Convened its first classes January 9, 1866, in former Union Army barracks in Nashville under the American Missionary Association. In 1871 its Jubilee Singers toured to save the school, carrying spirituals to concert stages worldwide and funding Jubilee Hall; in 1930 Fisk became the first Black college accredited by SACS.

claims.citationLabel: University history page

claims.object: First African American institution accredited by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools, 1930
claims.citationLabel: University history page

entities.locationLabel: Fisk University campus, North Nashville
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1866. Cited from Fisk University.
first in nation: First African American institution accredited by the Southern Association of Colleges and Schools, 1930. Cited from Fisk University.

entities.historicalContext: Fisk exemplified the liberal-arts wing of the freedmen's colleges, insisting from the start on a classical curriculum. Its campus later became a hub of the Harlem Renaissance generation, with W. E. B. Du Bois among its earliest graduates.

entities.summary: Founded February 14, 1867, as the Augusta Institute in the basement of Springfield Baptist Church in Augusta, Georgia, to educate Black men for ministry and teaching. Relocated to Atlanta in 1879 and renamed Morehouse in 1913, it remains the nation's only historically Black liberal arts college for men.

claims.object: Rev. William Jefferson White, at Springfield Baptist Church, Augusta
claims.citationLabel: College history page

entities.locationLabel: Morehouse campus, Atlanta University Center, West End
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1867. Cited from Morehouse College.
founded by: Rev. William Jefferson White, at Springfield Baptist Church, Augusta. Cited from Morehouse College.

entities.historicalContext: Morehouse grew within the Atlanta University Center, the densest concentration of Black higher education in the country. Its tradition of producing ministers, scholars, and public leaders reached worldwide visibility through alumnus Martin Luther King Jr., class of 1948.

entities.summary: Founded in 1867 as the Centenary Biblical Institute by the Baltimore Conference of the Methodist Episcopal Church to train ministers. Renamed Morgan College in 1890 for trustee Rev. Lyttleton Morgan, it remained private until Maryland purchased it in 1939 to widen public higher education for Black citizens.

claims.citationLabel: University history page

claims.object: Baltimore Conference of the Methodist Episcopal Church
claims.citationLabel: University history page

entities.locationLabel: Morgan State campus, Northeast Baltimore
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1867. Cited from Morgan State University.
founded by: Baltimore Conference of the Methodist Episcopal Church. Cited from Morgan State University.

entities.historicalContext: Morgan traces the common arc of many HBCUs, from denominational seminary to teacher training to state university. Its 1939 purchase by Maryland reflected growing pressure on states to fund Black public higher education in the decades before Brown.

entities.summary: Founded April 1, 1868, on the Virginia Peninsula by Brig. Gen. Samuel Chapman Armstrong with American Missionary Association backing. Its normal-and-agricultural model of learning by doing trained thousands of teachers for freedpeople's schools, among them Booker T. Washington, class of 1875.

entities.locationLabel: Hampton University campus, waterfront on Hampton Roads
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1868. Cited from Hampton University.
founded by: Brig. Gen. Samuel Chapman Armstrong. Cited from Hampton University.

entities.historicalContext: Hampton stood at the center of the debate over industrial versus classical education for the freedpeople, and its campus sits near the Emancipation Oak, where the Emancipation Proclamation was read to the local Black community. Its teaching model was carried across the South by graduates who founded schools of their own.

entities.summary: Formed July 1, 1988, by consolidating Atlanta University, founded 1865 as the nation's first institution to award graduate degrees to African Americans, with Clark College, founded 1869 as the first four-year liberal arts college serving a primarily Black student body. W. E. B. Du Bois taught on the Atlanta University faculty.

claims.citationLabel: University history page

claims.object: Parent institution Atlanta University, 1865, was the nation's first to award graduate degrees to African Americans
claims.citationLabel: University history page

entities.locationLabel: Clark Atlanta campus, Atlanta University Center
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1988. Cited from Clark Atlanta University.
first in nation: Parent institution Atlanta University, 1865, was the nation's first to award graduate degrees to African Americans. Cited from Clark Atlanta University.

entities.historicalContext: The 1988 consolidation joined two Reconstruction-era institutions that had shared the Atlanta University Center for a century. Atlanta University's sociology school, led for years by Du Bois, produced some of the earliest systematic scholarship on Black life in America.

entities.summary: Founded in 1876 as the Medical Department of Central Tennessee College with a $30,000 gift from Samuel Meharry and his brothers, repaying a debt of kindness to a formerly enslaved family. It was the first medical school for African Americans in the South and received its independent charter in 1915.

entities.locationLabel: Meharry campus, North Nashville, adjacent to Fisk University
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1876. Cited from Meharry Medical College.
first in nation: First medical school for African Americans in the South. Cited from Meharry Medical College.

entities.historicalContext: For most of the twentieth century Meharry and Howard together trained the majority of Black physicians and dentists in the United States. Its graduates staffed clinics and hospitals in communities that segregated medicine otherwise left unserved.

entities.summary: Established August 14, 1876, by the Texas Legislature as the Agricultural and Mechanical College of Texas for Colored Youths, opening at Prairie View in 1878. It was the first state-supported college for African Americans in Texas and is the state's second-oldest public institution of higher education.

claims.object: First state-supported college in Texas for African Americans
claims.citationLabel: College history page

entities.locationLabel: Prairie View A&M campus, Waller County
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1876. Cited from Prairie View A&M University.
first in state: First state-supported college in Texas for African Americans. Cited from Prairie View A&M University.

entities.historicalContext: Prairie View was created during Reconstruction and later folded into the 1890 land-grant system, which required Southern states to fund Black colleges to keep receiving federal agricultural money. It anchored teacher training and agricultural extension for Black Texans for generations.

entities.summary: Chartered by Louisiana's Legislative Act 87 of 1880 through the efforts of Reconstruction-era leaders including P.B.S. Pinchback, T.T. Allain, and Henry Demas, opening in New Orleans with 12 students. Relocated in 1914 to a bluff above the Mississippi at Scotlandville, it anchors the nation's only HBCU system.

entities.locationLabel: Southern University campus, Scotlandville, on Scott's Bluff
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1880. Cited from Southern University and A&M College.
founded by: P.B.S. Pinchback, T.T. Allain, T.B. Stamps, and Henry Demas. Cited from Southern University and A&M College.

entities.historicalContext: Southern was willed into being by Black legislators who held office during Reconstruction, then sustained through the era that stripped their power. Its Baton Rouge campus became the flagship of a five-campus system unique among HBCUs.

entities.summary: Opened April 11, 1881, in the basement of Atlanta's Friendship Baptist Church by New England educators Sophia B. Packard and Harriet E. Giles as the Atlanta Baptist Female Seminary. Renamed for the Spelman family in 1884, it became the nation's foremost college for women of African descent.

entities.locationLabel: Spelman campus, Atlanta University Center, West End
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1881. Cited from Spelman College.
founded by: Sophia B. Packard and Harriet E. Giles. Cited from Spelman College.

entities.historicalContext: Spelman gave institutional form to the education of Black women at a moment when almost no college would admit them. Its early support from the Rockefeller family, honoring the abolitionist Spelmans, tied Northern philanthropy to Black women's higher education for decades.

entities.summary: Opened July 4, 1881, in a one-room shanty near Butler Chapel A.M.E. Zion Church with Booker T. Washington as founding principal. Students made the bricks and raised the buildings themselves; by Washington's death in 1915 the campus counted about 100 buildings, 1,500 students, and a $2 million endowment.

entities.locationLabel: Tuskegee University campus, Macon County
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1881. Cited from Tuskegee University.
founded by: Booker T. Washington, founding principal, 1881. Cited from National Park Service.

entities.historicalContext: Tuskegee became the most influential expression of self-help industrial education, and George Washington Carver's laboratory made it a center of agricultural science. The campus is a National Historic Site, and the adjacent airfield trained the Tuskegee Airmen of World War II.

entities.summary: Opened October 3, 1887, as the State Normal College for Colored Students in Tallahassee with 15 students and two instructors under founding president Thomas DeSaille Tucker. Designated Florida's land-grant institution for Black students in 1891 under the Second Morrill Act.

entities.locationLabel: FAMU campus, on the highest hill in Tallahassee
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1887. Cited from Florida A&M University.
land grant designation: Florida's land-grant institution for African Americans under the Second Morrill Act, 1891. Cited from Florida A&M University.

entities.historicalContext: FAMU grew from a normal school into one of the largest HBCUs in the country, adding law, pharmacy, and graduate schools in the 1950s. Its students helped lead the 1956 Tallahassee bus boycott, an early campaign of the Southern civil rights movement.

entities.summary: Founded March 9, 1891, as the Agricultural and Mechanical College for the Colored Race under the Second Morrill Act, moving from Raleigh to donated land in Greensboro in 1893. On February 1, 1960, four freshmen remembered as the A&T Four began the Greensboro sit-ins at a downtown Woolworth lunch counter.

entities.locationLabel: N.C. A&T campus, East Market Street, Greensboro
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1891. Cited from North Carolina A&T State University.
notable event: Four A&T freshmen launched the Greensboro sit-ins on February 1, 1960. Cited from North Carolina A&T State University.

entities.historicalContext: A&T was the nation's second land-grant college for Black students under the 1890 Morrill Act and grew into the largest HBCU in the country. The sit-in movement its students ignited spread to dozens of cities within weeks and helped found SNCC.

entities.summary: Founded in New Orleans by St. Katharine Drexel and the Sisters of the Blessed Sacrament, opening as a high school in 1915 and establishing its College of Liberal Arts and Sciences in 1925. It is the nation's only historically Black and Catholic university and a leading source of Black graduates who earn medical degrees.

claims.citationLabel: The Full Story history page

entities.locationLabel: Xavier campus, Gert Town, along Washington Avenue and Pine Street
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the only or oldest surviving example of its kind in a defined scope (oldest Black-owned business in a city, only remaining Rosenwald school in a county) with evidence supporting the superlative claim.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1925. Cited from Xavier University of Louisiana.
first in nation: Only historically Black and Catholic university in the United States. Cited from Xavier University of Louisiana.

entities.historicalContext: Xavier joined the later wave of HBCU founding, built by Catholic religious philanthropy rather than Protestant missionary societies or state legislatures. Its pharmacy college, opened in 1927, and premedical programs became national pipelines into the health professions.

entities.summary: Chartered June 6, 1930, by merging two Reconstruction-era institutions, Straight University, founded 1869, and New Orleans University, founded 1873, and named for education reformer James Hardy Dillard. Its oak-lined Gentilly campus of white buildings opened to students in 1935.

claims.citationLabel: History and traditions page

claims.object: Straight University, 1869, and New Orleans University, 1873

entities.locationLabel: Dillard campus, Gentilly Boulevard
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1930. Cited from Dillard University.
formed by merger: Straight University, 1869, and New Orleans University, 1873. Cited from Dillard University.

entities.historicalContext: Dillard consolidated the Congregationalist and Methodist strands of New Orleans's freedmen's colleges into a single modern university. The merger, backed by the Rosenwald Fund and local philanthropy, typified the interwar reorganization of Black higher education.

entities.summary: Founded in 1865 as the Raleigh Institute by the American Baptist Home Mission Society, Shaw became the first historically Black college in the American South. Its Estey Hall (1873) was among the first college dormitories for African American women, and its Leonard Medical School (1881) and law school (1888) were early professional programs for Black students.

claims.citationLabel: A History of Progress

claims.object: First historically Black college in the American South
claims.citationLabel: A History of Progress

entities.locationLabel: Shaw University campus, downtown Raleigh
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1865. Cited from Shaw University.
first in region: First historically Black college in the American South. Cited from Shaw University.

entities.historicalContext: Shaw's downtown Raleigh campus became a seedbed of civil rights organizing; the 1960 conference on campus that produced the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee linked student activism to the wider movement. Its medical and legal schools, closed in the twentieth century, had trained generations of Black professionals when Southern white institutions barred their admission.

entities.summary: Established in 1873 in the basement of Warnersville Methodist Episcopal Church in Greensboro, North Carolina, Bennett began as a coeducational school for freedpeople. In 1926 it became a college for women, one of only two historically Black women's colleges in the United States.

claims.citationLabel: College history page

claims.object: Reorganized as a college for women in 1926
claims.citationLabel: College history page

entities.locationLabel: Bennett College campus, east Greensboro
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1873. Cited from Bennett College.
became womens college: Reorganized as a college for women in 1926. Cited from Bennett College.

entities.historicalContext: Bennett's survival through Reconstruction-era poverty, Jim Crow, and twentieth-century accreditation battles made it a pillar of Black women's higher education in the Piedmont. Its graduates, known as Bennett Belles, carried teacher training and leadership into schools and civic life across the South.

entities.summary: Chartered in 1869 and named for Massachusetts governor William Claflin and his father, Claflin was South Carolina's first college open to all students regardless of race, sex, or religion. Its 1882 graduates Nathaniel Middleton and William Bulkley were among the earliest African Americans to complete a four-year collegiate course in the state.

claims.citationLabel: University history timeline

claims.object: First South Carolina university open to all students regardless of race, complexion, or religious opinion
claims.citationLabel: University history timeline

entities.locationLabel: Claflin University campus, Orangeburg
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1869. Cited from Claflin University.
first in state: First South Carolina university open to all students regardless of race, complexion, or religious opinion. Cited from Claflin University.

entities.historicalContext: Claflin's Methodist roots and Reconstruction-era charter placed it at the center of Orangeburg's Black institutional life. The 1872 Morrill land-grant department on its campus later separated to become South Carolina State University, splitting teacher and agricultural training between two HBCU neighbors.

entities.summary: Philanthropist Bathsheba A. Benedict of Rhode Island helped found Benedict Institute on an eighty-acre Columbia, South Carolina plantation in 1870 through the American Baptist Home Mission Society. The South Carolina legislature chartered it as Benedict College in 1894, expanding from teacher and preacher training into a liberal arts institution.

claims.object: South Carolina legislature chartered Benedict College on November 2, 1894

entities.locationLabel: Benedict College campus, Columbia
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1870. Cited from Benedict College.
chartered as college: South Carolina legislature chartered Benedict College on November 2, 1894. Cited from Benedict College.

entities.historicalContext: Benedict opened in a former slaveholder's mansion with tuition-free instruction for freedpeople when public schooling for Black South Carolinians barely existed. Its partnership with the Baptist Education and Missionary Convention of South Carolina sustained the college through decades when state support for Black higher education was minimal.

entities.summary: The Christian Methodist Episcopal Church opened the CME High School in Jackson, Tennessee, on November 12, 1882, under principal Jennie E. Lane and founder Bishop Isaac Lane. A college department organized in 1896 led the Board of Trustees to rename the institution Lane College.

entities.locationLabel: Lane College campus, east Jackson
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1882. Cited from Lane College.
founded by: Christian Methodist Episcopal Church under Bishop Isaac Lane. Cited from Lane College.

entities.historicalContext: Lane was the first four-year institution established by the CME Church, extending denominational education beyond the seminary model. Its Jackson campus served West Tennessee's Black communities when segregated public colleges barred most African American applicants.

entities.summary: Tracing to Lincoln Chapel at Camp Shiloh in 1862, the American Missionary Association school became LeMoyne Normal and Commercial School in 1871 after abolitionist Francis J. LeMoyne's gift. It merged with Owen Junior College in 1968, forming Memphis's only historically Black college.

claims.citationLabel: About LeMoyne-Owen College

claims.object: LeMoyne College and Owen Junior College merged in 1968
claims.citationLabel: About LeMoyne-Owen College

entities.locationLabel: LeMoyne-Owen College campus, Soulsville, Walker Avenue
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1862. Cited from LeMoyne-Owen College.
merged: LeMoyne College and Owen Junior College merged in 1968. Cited from LeMoyne-Owen College.

entities.historicalContext: Rebuilt after the 1866 Memphis race massacre destroyed its first building, LeMoyne educated teachers and professionals through yellow fever epidemics and segregation. Its Walker Avenue campus sits in Soulsville, near Stax Records, linking the college to Memphis's Black cultural and musical history.

entities.summary: The Freedmen's Aid Society of the Methodist Episcopal Church founded Wiley near Marshall, Texas, in 1873 to educate Black youth in the arts and sciences. By 1880 the college occupied its present fifty-five-acre campus and became the first Black college west of the Mississippi River.

claims.citationLabel: About Wiley University

claims.object: First Black college west of the Mississippi River
claims.citationLabel: About Wiley University

entities.locationLabel: Wiley University campus, Marshall
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1873. Cited from Wiley University.
first in region: First Black college west of the Mississippi River. Cited from Wiley University.

entities.historicalContext: Wiley's debate team gained national attention in the 1930s when students challenged Jim Crow restrictions on interracial competition. The Marshall campus anchored higher education for East Texas Black communities when state universities remained closed to them.

entities.summary: The American Baptist Home Mission Society began classes for freedpeople in Richmond in 1865, opening Richmond Theological School for Freedmen near the site of Lumpkin's Jail. Richmond Theological Institute merged with Wayland Seminary in 1899 to form Virginia Union University on its Lombardy Street campus.

entities.locationLabel: Virginia Union campus, Northside Richmond, Lombardy Street
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1865. Cited from Virginia Union University.
formed by merger: Richmond Theological Institute and Wayland Seminary merged in 1899 as Virginia Union University. Cited from Virginia Union University.

entities.historicalContext: Virginia Union's early classes met in buildings that had confined enslaved people, transforming a site of bondage into a campus of learning. Hartshorn Memorial College, a women's school merged in 1932, and later Storer College strengthened its mission of training Black clergy, teachers, and civic leaders.

entities.summary: School #1 opened January 9, 1865, in Baltimore's African Baptist Church through the Baltimore Association for the Moral and Educational Improvement of the Colored People. Relocated to Bowie, Maryland, in 1911, it is Maryland's oldest historically Black college and among the ten oldest HBCUs in the country.

claims.citationLabel: University history page

claims.object: Maryland's first historically Black college or university

entities.locationLabel: Bowie State University campus, Prince George's County
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1865. Cited from Bowie State University.
first in state: Maryland's first historically Black college or university. Cited from Bowie State University.

entities.historicalContext: Born when Maryland refused to fund schools for Black citizens, Bowie's normal-school mission supplied teachers to segregated classrooms for decades before expanding into a university system member. Its Baltimore founding site is marked by Bowie State University Way at Calvert and Saratoga streets.

entities.summary: Baltimore's Colored High School hosted a one-year teacher training course in 1900 that grew into Fanny Jackson Coppin Normal School by 1926. Named for the Oberlin-educated educator and missionary, Coppin became a four-year teachers college in 1938 and a university in 2004.

claims.object: Renamed Fanny Jackson Coppin Normal School in 1926
claims.citationLabel: CSU History

entities.locationLabel: Coppin State campus, West North Avenue, Baltimore
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1900. Cited from Coppin State University.
named for: Renamed Fanny Jackson Coppin Normal School in 1926. Cited from Coppin State University.

entities.historicalContext: Coppin emerged from Baltimore's public school system to train Black elementary teachers when professional credentials were gatekept by race. Its West North Avenue campus anchors education and nursing programs focused on Baltimore's central city and surrounding metropolitan communities.

entities.summary: Ohio's General Assembly created the Combined Normal and Industrial Department at Wilberforce in 1887, a state-supported teacher and vocational program alongside the private A.M.E. Wilberforce University. Separated in 1947 and renamed Central State College in 1951, it is Ohio's only public historically Black university and an 1890 land-grant institution.

claims.citationLabel: Our History page

claims.object: Ohio's only public historically Black university
claims.citationLabel: 2024–2025 Fact Book

entities.locationLabel: Central State University campus, Greene County
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the only or oldest surviving example of its kind in a defined scope (oldest Black-owned business in a city, only remaining Rosenwald school in a county) with evidence supporting the superlative claim.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1887. Cited from Central State University.
first in state: Ohio's only public historically Black university. Cited from Central State University.

entities.historicalContext: Central State shares the Wilberforce hilltop with Wilberforce University but followed a distinct path as a state-funded normal and industrial school. A 1974 tornado devastated the campus; rebuilding preserved its role as a Midwestern land-grant HBCU training educators, scientists, and musicians.

entities.summary: Kentucky chartered the State Normal School for Colored Persons on May 18, 1886, locating it in Frankfort after the city donated funds and a bluff-top site. Classes began October 11, 1887, in Recitation Hall — now Jackson Hall — under first president John Henry Jackson.

claims.citationLabel: History of Kentucky State University

claims.object: First session opened October 11, 1887, with 55 students
claims.citationLabel: History of Kentucky State University

entities.locationLabel: Kentucky State University campus, Frankfort bluff
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1886. Cited from Kentucky State University.
opened: First session opened October 11, 1887, with 55 students. Cited from Kentucky State University.

entities.historicalContext: Kentucky State was the Commonwealth's second state-supported higher education institution and later its 1890 land-grant college for Black students. Frankfort's civic campaign to host the normal school reflected Reconstruction-era ambitions to train teachers for segregated Black schools across the Bluegrass region.

entities.summary: The North Louisiana Colored Agriculture Relief Association opened the Colored Industrial and Agricultural School on November 1, 1901, in Grambling, Louisiana. Booker T. Washington sent Charles P. Adams to organize the school; Adams served as founding president for thirty-six years as it grew into a state university.

entities.locationLabel: Grambling State University campus, Lincoln Parish
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1901. Cited from Grambling State University.
founded by: North Louisiana Colored Agriculture Relief Association with Charles P. Adams as founding president. Cited from Grambling State University.

entities.historicalContext: Grambling began as a farmer-led industrial school in rural north Louisiana and became a teacher-training hub under state support. Its marching band and football program later brought global visibility to a campus rooted in community self-help and Tuskegee-inspired vocational education.

entities.summary: Walden Seminary opened in Little Rock in 1877 as the Little Rock Annual Conference's school for freedmen, the first such effort west of the Mississippi River. Renamed Philander Smith College in 1882 after a gift from Adeline Smith, it received a four-year charter in 1883.

claims.citationLabel: 2022–2025 College Catalogue

claims.object: First attempt west of the Mississippi River to provide higher education to freedmen
claims.citationLabel: 2022–2025 College Catalogue

entities.locationLabel: Philander Smith campus, Daisy Bates Drive, Little Rock
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1877. Cited from Philander Smith College.
first in region: First attempt west of the Mississippi River to provide higher education to freedmen. Cited from Philander Smith College.

entities.historicalContext: Philander Smith grew from a Methodist seminary in Wesley Chapel into a liberal arts college near the Arkansas state capitol. Its graduates joined Little Rock's civil rights struggles and professional ranks when Arkansas's public universities remained segregated into the mid-twentieth century.

entities.summary: A.M.E. Zion ministers founded Zion Wesley Institute in Concord, North Carolina, in 1879 to train clergy. After relocating to Salisbury in 1882 on J. M. Gray's Delta Grove farm, the legislature renamed it Livingstone College in 1887 for missionary David Livingstone.

claims.citationLabel: About Livingstone College

entities.locationLabel: Livingstone College campus, Salisbury
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1879. Cited from Livingstone College.
renamed: Renamed Livingstone College in 1887. Cited from Livingstone College.

entities.historicalContext: Joseph Charles Price, Livingstone's first president, became a nationally known advocate for Black higher education through A.M.E. Zion networks. The Salisbury campus, adjacent to Hood Theological Seminary, sustained church-related liberal arts training in the Piedmont when public options for Black students were scarce.

entities.summary: African Methodist Episcopal preachers founded the Connectional School for the Education of Negro Youth in Austin, Texas, on April 4, 1872. The school moved to Waco in 1877, received a state charter as Paul Quinn College in 1881, and relocated to Dallas in 1990.

claims.citationLabel: Mission, Vision and History

claims.object: Chartered by the State of Texas and renamed Paul Quinn College in 1881
claims.citationLabel: WE Over Me: The Legacy of Paul Quinn College

entities.locationLabel: Paul Quinn College campus, southern Dallas
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1872. Cited from Paul Quinn College.
chartered: Chartered by the State of Texas and renamed Paul Quinn College in 1881. Cited from Paul Quinn College.

entities.historicalContext: Paul Quinn is among the oldest HBCUs west of the Mississippi, beginning in a church basement when Texas freedom was still uncertain. Its move to Dallas and later urban-work-college model extended A.M.E. Church education from Reconstruction trade training to twenty-first-century community development.

entities.summary: Opened by Troy University on December 1, 2000 — forty-five years to the day after Rosa Parks's arrest — on the downtown Montgomery block where that arrest took place, at the former Empire Theater site. Its permanent exhibit reconstructs December 1, 1955 and the 381-day bus boycott that followed, including a restored city bus of the era.

built at site of: Rosa Parks's December 1, 1955 arrest (former Empire Theater block). Cited from Troy University — Rosa Parks Museum, visitor information.

entities.historicalContext: Parks's arrest on a Montgomery city bus triggered the 1955-56 Montgomery Bus Boycott, the mass protest that brought Martin Luther King Jr. to national prominence and led to Browder v. Gayle, which struck down bus segregation.

entities.summary: A 1914 burlesque house at 253 West 125th Street that reopened to Black performers and audiences in 1934, the year its Amateur Night began — a 17-year-old Ella Fitzgerald won that November. For the next four decades the Apollo was the proving ground of Black popular music, from swing and bebop to R&B and soul.

entities.locationLabel: 253 West 125th Street, Harlem, Manhattan

notabilityBasis.note: opened to black audiences year: 1934. Cited from Apollo Theater — official history.
launched program: Amateur Night at the Apollo, 1934. Cited from Apollo Theater — about.

entities.historicalContext: The Apollo anchored 125th Street as the main stage of Black entertainment during and after the Harlem Renaissance; it was designated a New York City landmark and listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1983.

entities.displayName: Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture
entities.nameLower: schomburg center for research in black culture
entities.summary: Founded in 1925 as the Division of Negro Literature, History and Prints at the New York Public Library's 135th Street Branch. In 1926 the library acquired bibliophile Arturo Alfonso Schomburg's roughly 5,000-item personal collection, the seed of what is now one of the world's leading research archives of the global Black experience.

entities.locationLabel: 515 Malcolm X Boulevard, Harlem, Manhattan
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1925. Cited from New York Public Library — Schomburg Center.
acquired founding collection: Arturo Schomburg's personal collection of about 5,000 items, 1926. Cited from New York Public Library — Schomburg Center.

entities.historicalContext: Schomburg, a Puerto Rican-born Black scholar, assembled his collection to refute claims that people of African descent had no history; the center that bears his name became a cornerstone of the Harlem Renaissance's intellectual life and was renamed for him in 1972.

entities.displayName: National Museum of African American History and Culture
entities.nameLower: national museum of african american history and culture
entities.summary: Established by Act of Congress in 2003 and opened on the National Mall on September 24, 2016 as the Smithsonian's nineteenth museum. Its collection of more than 40,000 objects ranges from Harriet Tubman's hymnal to a segregated Southern Railway passenger car, presented in a bronze-latticed building by David Adjaye.

claims.citationLabel: Official Smithsonian page
Official Smithsonian page

entities.locationLabel: 1400 Constitution Avenue NW, National Mall
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: established by congress year: 2003. Cited from Smithsonian — NMAAHC, about the museum.
opened year: 2016. Cited from Smithsonian — NMAAHC, about the museum.

entities.historicalContext: Proposals for a national museum of Black history circulated for nearly a century — Black Civil War veterans first petitioned for a memorial building in 1915 — before the 2003 legislation signed by President George W. Bush created the museum.

entities.displayName: DuSable Black History Museum and Education Center
entities.nameLower: dusable black history museum and education center
entities.summary: Chartered on February 16, 1961 by artist and educator Margaret Taylor-Burroughs, her husband Charles, and four co-founders, opening in the Burroughs home on Chicago's South Side. One of the oldest independent museums of Black history in the United States, it was renamed in 1968 for Jean Baptiste Point DuSable, Chicago's Black founder.

claims.object: Jean Baptiste Point DuSable, first non-Indigenous permanent settler of Chicago

entities.locationLabel: 740 East 56th Place, Washington Park, Chicago
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

named for: Jean Baptiste Point DuSable, first non-Indigenous permanent settler of Chicago. Cited from DuSable Black History Museum — about.

entities.historicalContext: Founded outside white-led museum institutions during the civil rights era, the DuSable modeled community-built Black museology; Taylor-Burroughs, a poet and printmaker of the Chicago Black Renaissance, ran it from her own living room before it moved to Washington Park in 1973.

entities.summary: Founded in 1985 by Esther Gordy Edwards in the house at 2648 West Grand Boulevard where her brother Berry Gordy ran Motown Records from 1959. The museum preserves Studio A — kept nearly as it was when the Supremes, Temptations, Miracles, and Stevie Wonder recorded there — along with Gordy's upstairs apartment.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

preserves site: Studio A and the original Hitsville U.S.A. headquarters of Motown Records (est. 1959). Cited from Motown Museum — visit.

entities.historicalContext: From this converted photographer's studio, Motown became the most successful Black-owned record company of its era, producing a run of 1960s hits that carried Black artists to the center of American popular music.

entities.summary: Founded in 1968 as a museum for artists of African descent built around working studio space, it has run an artist-in-residence program from its first years; alumni include Kehinde Wiley and Njideka Akunyili Crosby. In November 2025 it reopened in a purpose-built seven-story home on West 125th Street designed by David Adjaye.

entities.locationLabel: 144 West 125th Street, Harlem, Manhattan
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1968. Cited from Studio Museum in Harlem — about.
runs program: Artist-in-Residence studio residency for emerging artists of African descent. Cited from Studio Museum in Harlem — about.

entities.historicalContext: Founded amid the Black Arts Movement, when major New York museums showed almost no work by Black artists, the Studio Museum put artists' working practice — not just finished objects — at the center of its mission.

entities.displayName: Charles H. Wright Museum of African American History
entities.nameLower: charles h. wright museum of african american history
entities.summary: Founded in 1965 by Dr. Charles H. Wright, a Detroit obstetrician, as the International Afro-American Museum. Its 125,000-square-foot building on East Warren Avenue, opened in 1997, holds more than 35,000 artifacts and the long-running core exhibition 'And Still We Rise: Our Journey Through African American History and Culture.'

claims.citationLabel: Official museum site

claims.citationLabel: Official museum site

entities.locationLabel: 315 East Warren Avenue, Midtown Detroit
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1965. Cited from The Wright Museum — about.
collection size: More than 35,000 artifacts and archival materials. Cited from The Wright Museum — about.

entities.historicalContext: Wright founded the museum after visiting a Danish memorial to World War II resistance fighters, determined that Black history deserved the same permanent institutional care; for decades it was the largest African American history museum in the country.

entities.summary: Founded in 1990 in Kansas City's 18th & Vine district — blocks from where the Negro National League was organized in 1920 — by a group including Kansas City Monarchs first baseman Buck O'Neil, who chaired its board. Its Field of Legends gallery fields life-size bronzes of league greats on a scaled diamond.

located in district: Historic 18th & Vine district, center of Black Kansas City in the early 20th century. Cited from Negro Leagues Baseball Museum.

entities.historicalContext: The Negro Leagues, organized in 1920 at Kansas City's Paseo YMCA under Rube Foster, sustained professional Black baseball through four decades of segregation and produced players — Satchel Paige, Josh Gibson, Jackie Robinson — now recognized among the game's greatest.

entities.summary: Opened September 28, 1991 at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, where Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated on April 4, 1968 while supporting the city's striking sanitation workers. Room 306 is preserved, and the exhibits trace the movement from slavery and Jim Crow through the Montgomery boycott, the sit-ins, and Memphis 1968.

claims.object: Lorraine Motel, site of the April 4, 1968 assassination of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.

entities.locationLabel: 450 Mulberry Street, Memphis (Lorraine Motel)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: opened year: 1991. Cited from National Civil Rights Museum — about.
preserves site: Lorraine Motel, site of the April 4, 1968 assassination of Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.. Cited from National Civil Rights Museum — about.

entities.historicalContext: The Lorraine was one of the few Memphis motels serving Black travelers under segregation and a regular stop for touring musicians and movement leaders; King came to Memphis in 1968 to stand with sanitation workers striking under the banner 'I Am a Man.'

entities.summary: Opened in November 1992 in Birmingham's Civil Rights District, facing Kelly Ingram Park and a block from 16th Street Baptist Church, where four girls were killed in the 1963 Klan bombing. A Smithsonian Affiliate, it holds oral histories and archives of the 1963 Birmingham campaign, from the Children's Crusade to the fire hoses.

claims.object: Kelly Ingram Park and 16th Street Baptist Church

entities.locationLabel: 520 16th Street North, Civil Rights District
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

located adjacent to: Kelly Ingram Park and 16th Street Baptist Church. Cited from Birmingham Civil Rights Institute.

entities.historicalContext: Birmingham's 1963 demonstrations — met with police dogs and fire hoses in Kelly Ingram Park — and the September bombing of 16th Street Baptist Church helped move national opinion toward the Civil Rights Act of 1964.

entities.summary: Founded in 1976 during the Bicentennial, the AAMP was the first museum funded and built by a major U.S. municipality to preserve and interpret African American heritage. Its galleries at 701 Arch Street stand in the historic district where Philadelphia's free Black community organized churches, lodges, and abolition societies.

notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1976. Cited from African American Museum in Philadelphia — about.
first of kind: First museum funded and built by a major U.S. city to preserve African American heritage. Cited from African American Museum in Philadelphia — about.

entities.historicalContext: Philadelphia held the largest free Black population of the early republic; Richard Allen's Mother Bethel AME Church and the Free African Society were founded blocks from the museum's site, a legacy its core exhibition 'Audacious Freedom' documents.

entities.summary: Founded in Milwaukee in 1988 by Dr. James Cameron, who survived a 1930 lynching in Marion, Indiana, and devoted his life to documenting that history. Closed in 2008 after his death, it continued as a virtual museum from 2012 and reopened in February 2022 in Bronzeville, pairing an unsparing record of racial terror with galleries on resistance, repair, and Black achievement.

claims.citationSource: America's Black Holocaust Museum — history and impact
claims.citationLabel: Official museum history

claims.citationLabel: NPS article

entities.locationLabel: 401 West North Avenue, Bronzeville, Milwaukee
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded year: 1988. Cited from America's Black Holocaust Museum — history and impact.
reopened year: 2022. Cited from National Park Service — America's Black Holocaust Museum.

entities.historicalContext: Cameron, sixteen when a mob killed his two companions in 1930, wrote 'A Time of Terror' and built the museum to tell the full history of racial violence in America alongside the story of survival and repair; the rebuilt museum stands on the footprint of its former home.

entities.summary: A 1752 indigo and sugar estate on Louisiana's River Road, opened as a museum in December 2014 by New Orleans attorney John Cummings after some fifteen years of research. It was the first plantation museum in Louisiana centered on the lives of the enslaved, with memorials naming the more than 100,000 people enslaved in the state.

claims.object: Museum of slavery centered on the lives of the enslaved people of the estate and of Louisiana
claims.citationLabel: Official history page

entities.locationLabel: 5099 Highway 18 (River Road), Wallace, St. John the Baptist Parish
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: opened as museum year: 2014. Cited from Whitney Plantation.
dedicated focus: Museum of slavery centered on the lives of the enslaved people of the estate and of Louisiana. Cited from Whitney Plantation — history.

entities.historicalContext: The German Coast plantations along this stretch of the Mississippi were worked by thousands of enslaved people and lay at the heart of the 1811 German Coast uprising, the largest revolt of enslaved people in U.S. history; Whitney's memorials draw on Depression-era WPA interviews with formerly enslaved Louisianans.

entities.summary: Opened August 3, 2004 on Cincinnati's Ohio River bank — the water that divided slave states from free soil. Its centerpiece is a two-story 1830s log slave pen moved from a Kentucky farm, and its exhibits connect Underground Railroad history, from local conductors like Levi Coffin's network, to modern struggles against unfreedom.

claims.citationLabel: Official museum site

claims.object: North bank of the Ohio River, a historic crossing point from slavery to free soil

claims.citationLabel: Official visitor page

entities.locationLabel: 50 East Freedom Way, The Banks, Cincinnati
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: opened year: 2004. Cited from National Underground Railroad Freedom Center.
located on: North bank of the Ohio River, a historic crossing point from slavery to free soil. Cited from National Underground Railroad Freedom Center — visit.

entities.historicalContext: Thousands of freedom seekers crossed the Ohio River near Cincinnati before the Civil War, sheltered by Black and white abolitionist networks in the city's riverfront neighborhoods; the museum stands deliberately at that shoreline.

entities.summary: Opened June 27, 2023 on Gadsden's Wharf in Charleston, where an estimated 100,000 enslaved Africans were disembarked between 1783 and 1808. The building is raised on pillars above the wharf ground, and its Center for Family History supports African American genealogy back through the records of slavery.

claims.object: Gadsden's Wharf, a principal disembarkation point of the transatlantic slave trade

entities.locationLabel: 14 Wharfside Street, Gadsden's Wharf, Charleston
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

built at site of: Gadsden's Wharf, a principal disembarkation point of the transatlantic slave trade. Cited from International African American Museum — about.

entities.historicalContext: Charleston was the largest port of entry for enslaved Africans in North America; Gadsden's Wharf, most active in the final legal years of the trade, is among the most significant single sites of that history, which the museum spent two decades of planning to mark.

entities.displayName: Reginald F. Lewis Museum of Maryland African American History and Culture
entities.nameLower: reginald f. lewis museum of maryland african american history and culture
entities.summary: Opened in June 2005 near Baltimore's Inner Harbor and named for Reginald F. Lewis, the Baltimore-born financier who became the first African American to build a billion-dollar company, TLC Beatrice International. A Smithsonian Affiliate, it is Maryland's largest museum of African American history, holding the state's stories from slavery through the present.

named for: Reginald F. Lewis, first African American to build a billion-dollar company. Cited from Reginald F. Lewis Museum — about.

entities.historicalContext: Maryland's Black history runs from Frederick Douglass and Harriet Tubman, both born enslaved on the Eastern Shore, to Thurgood Marshall of Baltimore; the Lewis Foundation's $5 million gift to the museum's education programs gave the institution its name.

entities.summary: Organized in Harlem on August 25, 1925 under A. Philip Randolph, the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters became the first predominantly African American labor union chartered by the American Federation of Labor and the first to win a collective bargaining agreement with a major U.S. corporation, the Pullman Company, in 1937.
