entities.locationLabel: Harlem, New York City (union organizing center)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: held convention: In September 1940 Eleanor Roosevelt addressed the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters convention at the Harlem YMCA, where Randolph and porter delegates pressed for defense-industry jobs and desegregation of the armed forces. Cited from National Park Service.

founded: A. Philip Randolph and Pullman porters founded the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters in New York City on August 25, 1925 as the first predominantly African American labor union in the United States. Cited from Library of Congress.

entities.historicalContext: Twelve years of organizing preceded the Pullman Company's 1937 recognition of the BSCP; the union's Harlem base linked porters' shop-floor grievances to national campaigns against employment discrimination that Randolph later carried into the March on Washington Movement and the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom.

entities.summary: More than 1,200 Black union officials and rank-and-file members from thirty-seven national unions met at Chicago's LaSalle Hotel on September 23–24, 1972 to form the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, creating a permanent caucus to advance Black workers' interests inside the AFL-CIO.

claims.object: On September 23–24, 1972, more than 1,200 Black union officials and rank-and-file members from thirty-seven international and national unions met at the LaSalle Hotel in Chicago and established the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists as a permanent organization
claims.citationLabel: CBTU History

claims.object: Five Black labor leaders — William Lucy, Nelson Edwards, William Simons, Charles Hayes, and Cleveland Robinson — convened the founding conference after the AFL-CIO executive council took a neutral stance in the 1972 presidential election
claims.citationLabel: The Coalition of Black Trade Unionists is Formed

entities.locationLabel: Chicago, Illinois (founding conference site)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: On September 23–24, 1972, more than 1,200 Black union officials and rank-and-file members from thirty-seven international and national unions met at the LaSalle Hotel in Chicago and established the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists as a permanent organization. Cited from Coalition of Black Trade Unionists.

organized by: Five Black labor leaders — William Lucy, Nelson Edwards, William Simons, Charles Hayes, and Cleveland Robinson — convened the founding conference after the AFL-CIO executive council took a neutral stance in the 1972 presidential election. Cited from African American Registry.

entities.historicalContext: CBTU emerged when Black trade unionists concluded that AFL-CIO neutrality in 1972 would not protect Black workers from Nixon-era retrenchment; the Chicago meeting remains the largest single gathering of Black unionists in U.S. labor history and the group continues as an AFL-CIO constituency organization.

entities.summary: Black labor leaders convened the founding convention of the Negro American Labor Council at Detroit's Statler-Hilton Hotel on May 27–29, 1960, electing A. Philip Randolph president to press the AFL-CIO to end segregated locals and elect Black workers to union leadership.

claims.object: Dissatisfied with AFL-CIO President George Meany's civil-rights record, A. Philip Randolph and other African American labor leaders formed the Negro American Labor Council in 1960 to carry out the federation's civil-rights program from a Black workers' organization
claims.citationSource: The Martin Luther King, Jr. Research and Education Institute
claims.citationLabel: Negro American Labor Council (NALC)

claims.object: The NALC founding convention met May 27–29, 1960 at the Statler-Hilton Hotel in Detroit, electing Randolph president and Cleveland Robinson of District 65 as vice president
claims.citationLabel: NALC Founding Convention Program, Statler-Hilton Hotel, Detroit, 1960

entities.locationLabel: Detroit, Michigan (founding convention, Statler-Hilton Hotel)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: Dissatisfied with AFL-CIO President George Meany's civil-rights record, A. Philip Randolph and other African American labor leaders formed the Negro American Labor Council in 1960 to carry out the federation's civil-rights program from a Black workers' organization. Cited from The Martin Luther King, Jr. Research and Education Institute.

held convention: The NALC founding convention met May 27–29, 1960 at the Statler-Hilton Hotel in Detroit, electing Randolph president and Cleveland Robinson of District 65 as vice president. Cited from Walter P. Reuther Library.

entities.historicalContext: Randolph's NALC bridged the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters' labor tradition and the civil-rights movement; its 1961 recommendations to the AFL-CIO demanded desegregated locals, Black staff hires, and Black representation on policy-making bodies, and Randolph later chaired the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom.

entities.summary: In December 1869, 214 delegates met in Washington, D.C. to form the Colored National Labor Union as a counterpart to the white National Labor Union, petitioning Congress for land redistribution and credit for Black farmers excluded from most trade unions.

claims.object: In December 1869, 214 delegates attended the Colored National Labor Union convention in Washington, D.C., forming a national organization because white trade unions generally excluded Black workers
claims.citationLabel: African Americans and the American Labor Movement

claims.object: The 1869 convention petitioned Congress to subdivide public lands in the South into forty-acre farms and provide low-interest loans to Black farmers, and sent a further memorial in January 1871 on conditions in the southern states
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: 200 Years of Labor History

entities.locationLabel: Washington, D.C. (1869 convention city)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: In December 1869, 214 delegates attended the Colored National Labor Union convention in Washington, D.C., forming a national organization because white trade unions generally excluded Black workers. Cited from National Archives.

petitioned congress: The 1869 convention petitioned Congress to subdivide public lands in the South into forty-acre farms and provide low-interest loans to Black farmers, and sent a further memorial in January 1871 on conditions in the southern states. Cited from National Park Service.

entities.historicalContext: Isaac Myers, a Baltimore ship-caulker who had organized the Colored Caulkers Trade Union Society, served as the CNLU's president; though the union lasted only a few years, it documented Reconstruction-era Black workers' demand for both industrial organization and agrarian reform.

entities.summary: After garbage collectors Echol Cole and Robert Walker were crushed in a malfunctioning truck on February 1, 1968, roughly 1,300 mostly Black Memphis sanitation workers struck for sixty-five days, demanding AFSCME recognition, safer equipment, and wages above poverty levels.

claims.object: On February 12, 1968, about 1,300 Black sanitation workers employed by the Memphis Department of Public Works walked off the job after the deaths of Echol Cole and Robert Walker, demanding union recognition, safer trucks, and higher pay
claims.citationSource: The Martin Luther King, Jr. Research and Education Institute
claims.citationLabel: Memphis Sanitation Workers' Strike

claims.object: After a silent march of an estimated 42,000 supporters on April 8 and continued negotiations, the strike ended April 16, 1968 with city recognition of AFSCME Local 1733, wage increases, and a non-discrimination clause
claims.citationLabel: Memphis Sanitation Workers Went on Strike — Library of Congress

entities.locationLabel: Memphis, Tennessee (city hall and public-works strike center)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: began strike: On February 12, 1968, about 1,300 Black sanitation workers employed by the Memphis Department of Public Works walked off the job after the deaths of Echol Cole and Robert Walker, demanding union recognition, safer trucks, and higher pay. Cited from The Martin Luther King, Jr. Research and Education Institute.

settled: After a silent march of an estimated 42,000 supporters on April 8 and continued negotiations, the strike ended April 16, 1968 with city recognition of AFSCME Local 1733, wage increases, and a non-discrimination clause. Cited from Library of Congress.

entities.historicalContext: Strikers carried "I AM A MAN" placards that linked dignity to economic rights; Martin Luther King Jr. joined the cause as part of the Poor People's Campaign before his assassination in Memphis on April 4, 1968, and the settlement became a landmark public-sector organizing victory.

entities.summary: Black autoworkers at Chrysler's Dodge Main plant in Hamtramck, Michigan formed the Dodge Revolutionary Union Movement in May 1968 after a wildcat strike over speed-ups and racial job segregation, publishing the DRUM newsletter to challenge both Chrysler management and UAW Local 3 leadership.

claims.object: The Dodge Revolutionary Union Movement was formed in May 1968 at the Chrysler Dodge Main plant in Hamtramck, Michigan, among activists including General Baker, Mike Hamlin, John Watson, and Kenneth Cockrel
claims.citationLabel: Detroit Revolutionary Movements Records

claims.object: On May 2, 1968, DRUM staged a wildcat strike at Dodge Main demanding improvements for Black workers; a July 8, 1968 stoppage drew an estimated 4,000 workers out of the plant
claims.citationLabel: Dodge Revolutionary Union Movement — 12th Street Detroit

entities.locationLabel: Hamtramck, Michigan (Chrysler Dodge Main plant area)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: formed: The Dodge Revolutionary Union Movement was formed in May 1968 at the Chrysler Dodge Main plant in Hamtramck, Michigan, among activists including General Baker, Mike Hamlin, John Watson, and Kenneth Cockrel. Cited from Walter P. Reuther Library.

led wildcat strike: On May 2, 1968, DRUM staged a wildcat strike at Dodge Main demanding improvements for Black workers; a July 8, 1968 stoppage drew an estimated 4,000 workers out of the plant. Cited from Wayne State University.

entities.historicalContext: DRUM pioneered the Revolutionary Union Movement model — plant-based Black caucuses that used wildcat strikes and shop-floor newspapers when official unions would not address racial hierarchies on the assembly line; it became a core member of the League of Revolutionary Black Workers in 1969.

entities.summary: Formed in Detroit in 1969, the League of Revolutionary Black Workers united Dodge Revolutionary Union Movement chapters and related plant caucuses into a federation that coordinated wildcat strikes, propaganda, and challenges to UAW leadership over systemic racism in auto plants.

claims.object: In 1969 the League of Revolutionary Black Workers was created as the central organization for Revolutionary Union Movement groups formed after the 1968 DRUM wildcat strike at Dodge Main
claims.citationLabel: Detroit Revolutionary Movements Records — Reuther Library

claims.object: Factional infighting caused the League to split in 1971 and its influence subsequently declined, though its records document Black autoworkers' demands during the peak of the Black Power era
claims.citationLabel: Detroit Revolutionary Movements Records — finding aid

entities.locationLabel: Detroit, Michigan (League coordinating center)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: formed: In 1969 the League of Revolutionary Black Workers was created as the central organization for Revolutionary Union Movement groups formed after the 1968 DRUM wildcat strike at Dodge Main. Cited from Walter P. Reuther Library.

declined: Factional infighting caused the League to split in 1971 and its influence subsequently declined, though its records document Black autoworkers' demands during the peak of the Black Power era. Cited from Walter P. Reuther Library.

entities.historicalContext: The League extended the DRUM model to Eldon Avenue Gear and Lighting (ELRUM), Ford's River Rouge complex (FRUM), and non-auto workplaces including UPS and Detroit newspapers, forcing the UAW and Chrysler to confront Black workers' charges that union bureaucracy mirrored shop-floor racism.

entities.summary: Farmworkers in Shaw, Mississippi voted in April 1965 to form the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union and strike the Seligmann plantation, registering roughly one thousand members within two weeks and demanding a $1.25 minimum wage, an eight-hour day, and employer-paid medical coverage.

claims.object: In April 1965, forty-five Shaw farmworkers voted unanimously to found the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union with a drafted constitution and economic demands, then struck the Seligmann plantation
claims.citationLabel: Freedom to Resist: John Henry Sylvester and Strike City, Mississippi

claims.object: Within two weeks of the Shaw vote the union registered about one thousand members and more than two hundred workers were on strike across the Delta, calling for a $1.25 minimum wage, an eight-hour workday, and free medical coverage
claims.citationLabel: Project South: Freedom Labor Union (FLU)

entities.locationLabel: Shaw, Mississippi (Delta farmworker organizing center)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: In April 1965, forty-five Shaw farmworkers voted unanimously to found the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union with a drafted constitution and economic demands, then struck the Seligmann plantation. Cited from University of Southern Mississippi.

organized strike: Within two weeks of the Shaw vote the union registered about one thousand members and more than two hundred workers were on strike across the Delta, calling for a $1.25 minimum wage, an eight-hour workday, and free medical coverage. Cited from Stanford University Libraries.

entities.historicalContext: The MFLU grew from Freedom School conversations and support from the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and Delta Ministry, extending civil-rights organizing into plantation labor after the 1964 Civil Rights Act; strikers later established Strike City near Cleveland, Mississippi when evicted from plantation housing.

entities.summary: Black Workers for Justice formed in 1981 after Black women Kmart employees in Rocky Mount, North Carolina organized a boycott against race and gender discrimination; activists from ten counties met in Fremont in June 1982 to launch a statewide Black workers' organization.

claims.object: Black Workers for Justice formed in 1981 out of a struggle led by Black women workers at a Kmart store in Rocky Mount, North Carolina against race and gender discrimination
claims.citationLabel: About Us — Black Workers for Justice

claims.object: After a local Kmart boycott, Black workers and community activists from ten counties met at First Missionary Baptist Church in Fremont, North Carolina in June 1982 to establish BWFJ as a statewide organization
claims.citationLabel: Acting Locally and Globally: The Black Workers for Justice

entities.locationLabel: Rocky Mount, North Carolina (founding struggle city)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: formed: Black Workers for Justice formed in 1981 out of a struggle led by Black women workers at a Kmart store in Rocky Mount, North Carolina against race and gender discrimination. Cited from Black Workers for Justice.

held founding meeting: After a local Kmart boycott, Black workers and community activists from ten counties met at First Missionary Baptist Church in Fremont, North Carolina in June 1982 to establish BWFJ as a statewide organization. Cited from Facing South.

entities.historicalContext: Operating from a Workers Center in Rocky Mount, BWFJ later supported Schlage Lock plant workers fighting closure, families after the 1991 Imperial Foods fire in Hamlet, and the formation of UE Local 150 for North Carolina public-sector workers.

entities.summary: ILA Local 1422 in Charleston, South Carolina represents a waterfront workforce with deep roots in post-emancipation Black longshore organizing; the local gained national attention during the 2000 Charleston Five dispute over use of non-union labor at the port.

claims.object: ILA Local 1422 in Charleston represents longshore workers on the city's docks; the Avery Research Center documents a lineage from the 1869 Longshoremen's Protective Union Association of Black stevedores to the ILA local restored in 1936
claims.citationLabel: Longshoreman Strike 1866 — Avery Research Center

claims.object: Five Local 1422 members known as the Charleston Five faced felony riot charges after a January 19, 2000 confrontation on the docks over the Danish shipping company Nordana's use of non-union workers
claims.citationSource: College of Charleston Avery Research Center
claims.citationLabel: Charleston Five Collection

entities.locationLabel: Charleston, South Carolina (port longshore local)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: organized workforce: ILA Local 1422 in Charleston represents longshore workers on the city's docks; the Avery Research Center documents a lineage from the 1869 Longshoremen's Protective Union Association of Black stevedores to the ILA local restored in 1936. Cited from Avery Research Center.

faced prosecution: Five Local 1422 members known as the Charleston Five faced felony riot charges after a January 19, 2000 confrontation on the docks over the Danish shipping company Nordana's use of non-union workers. Cited from College of Charleston Avery Research Center.

entities.historicalContext: Charleston's Black longshoremen organized strikes for higher wages within months of emancipation and built durable union institutions through Reconstruction and Jim Crow; Local 1422 leaders including Kenneth Riley Jr. carried that waterfront tradition into twenty-first-century global solidarity campaigns.

entities.summary: In the summer of 1881, Black washerwomen in Atlanta's Summer Hill neighborhood formed the Washing Society and struck for a uniform rate of one dollar per dozen washes, growing to roughly three thousand members and threatening a general strike that forced concessions from the city's commercial elite.

claims.object: In summer 1881, Black washerwomen in Atlanta's Summer Hill district formed the Washing Society, set a standard rate of one dollar per dozen washes, and coordinated chapters across the city that grew to nearly three thousand members
claims.citationSource: UC Santa Barbara Undergraduate Journal of History
claims.citationLabel: The 1881 Atlanta Washerwoman Strike in the Pages of the Atlanta Constitution

claims.object: The washerwomen's coordinated action in 1881 grew so large that organizers threatened a general strike that would have shut Atlanta down, forcing the city's business and political establishment to negotiate
claims.citationLabel: Atlanta's Washerwomen Strike — Virginia State University

entities.locationLabel: Atlanta, Georgia (Summer Hill washerwomen organizing district)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: organized strike: In summer 1881, Black washerwomen in Atlanta's Summer Hill district formed the Washing Society, set a standard rate of one dollar per dozen washes, and coordinated chapters across the city that grew to nearly three thousand members. Cited from UC Santa Barbara Undergraduate Journal of History.

threatened general strike: The washerwomen's coordinated action in 1881 grew so large that organizers threatened a general strike that would have shut Atlanta down, forcing the city's business and political establishment to negotiate. Cited from Virginia State University.

entities.historicalContext: Less than two decades after emancipation, Atlanta's washerwomen used church networks and neighborhood committees to wage one of the most organized labor campaigns in the post-Reconstruction South, demonstrating how Black women domestic workers could translate collective power into wage standards.

entities.displayName: Federation of Southern Cooperatives/Land Assistance Fund
entities.nameLower: federation of southern cooperatives/land assistance fund
entities.summary: Established in 1967 to support Black farmers and rural communities across the South, the Federation of Southern Cooperatives opened its Rural Training and Research Center near Epes, Alabama in 1971 on land purchased with the Panola Land Buying Association, training members in cooperative management and land retention.

claims.object: The Federation's Rural Training and Research Center in Epes, Alabama opened in 1971 on a 374-acre tract purchased jointly with the Panola Land Buying Association to train low-income rural farmers in cooperative and farming practices
claims.citationLabel: FSC/LAF Rural Training and Research Center records

claims.object: Formed out of the civil rights movement, the Federation of Southern Cooperatives was established in 1967 to create community-based economic development for Black farmers and rural communities across the South
claims.citationLabel: CPCRS: Federation of Southern Cooperatives Rural Training & Research Center

entities.locationLabel: Rural Training and Research Center, Epes, Alabama
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: established training center: The Federation's Rural Training and Research Center in Epes, Alabama opened in 1971 on a 374-acre tract purchased jointly with the Panola Land Buying Association to train low-income rural farmers in cooperative and farming practices. Cited from Amistad Research Center.

founded: Formed out of the civil rights movement, the Federation of Southern Cooperatives was established in 1967 to create community-based economic development for Black farmers and rural communities across the South. Cited from University of Pennsylvania CPCRS.

entities.historicalContext: The Epes center served member cooperatives in more than a dozen states with workshops on cattle, greenhouse, and row-crop production while credit unions and revolving loan funds helped families resist the land loss that accelerated after the 1960s; the site is under study for National Register listing.

entities.summary: Incorporated in April 1966 in Alberta, Alabama near Gee's Bend, the Freedom Quilting Bee was a women's craft cooperative that grew from the civil rights movement to market quilts nationwide, with Estelle Witherspoon as president and more than sixty quilters at its first meeting in March 1966.

claims.object: The Freedom Quilting Bee cooperative was incorporated in April 1966 in Alberta, Alabama with Estelle Witherspoon as president after more than sixty quilters attended the group's first meeting in March 1966
claims.citationLabel: The Freedom Quilting Bee — Southern Changes

claims.object: Founded in 1966 out of the civil rights movement, the Bee created economic opportunities for African American women in Wilcox County by manufacturing and selling quilts nationwide
claims.citationLabel: Auburn University: Freedom Quilting Bee

entities.locationLabel: Alberta, Alabama (cooperative headquarters near Gee's Bend)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the first Black person, institution, or place to achieve, hold, found, or integrate something notable (a role, office, degree, business, record) — not merely an early or contemporaneous participant.

notabilityBasis.note: provided income: Founded in 1966 out of the civil rights movement, the Bee created economic opportunities for African American women in Wilcox County by manufacturing and selling quilts nationwide. Cited from Auburn University.

incorporated: The Freedom Quilting Bee cooperative was incorporated in April 1966 in Alberta, Alabama with Estelle Witherspoon as president after more than sixty quilters attended the group's first meeting in March 1966. Cited from Emory University.

entities.historicalContext: Rev. Francis X. Walter's interfaith civil-rights work in west Alabama linked quilters from Gee's Bend and Alberta to New York buyers; the cooperative gave Black Belt women income and organizational experience during voting-rights and land-rights struggles in one of the poorest counties in the United States.

entities.summary: Founded in New York City in 1932 as a pharmacists' union, Local 1199 expanded in the late 1950s into voluntary hospitals where a heavily African American and Puerto Rican workforce waged landmark strikes; Martin Luther King Jr. called 1199 his favorite union before it merged into SEIU in 1998.

claims.object: After organizing Montefiore Hospital in the late 1950s, Local 1199 launched large-scale campaigns among voluntary New York hospitals where a large proportion of workers were Black or Puerto Rican, including a forty-six-day strike beginning May 8, 1959
claims.citationLabel: Building a Union in the Hospitals: Local 1199 at Montefiore

claims.object: Martin Luther King Jr. famously described Local 1199 as his favorite union, and Coretta Scott King later served as honorary chair of the union's organizing campaigns as 1199 expanded beyond New York
claims.citationLabel: 1199SEIU United Healthcare Workers East — NYU Finding Aid

entities.locationLabel: Montefiore Medical Center area, Bronx, New York (early hospital organizing)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: organized hospitals: After organizing Montefiore Hospital in the late 1950s, Local 1199 launched large-scale campaigns among voluntary New York hospitals where a large proportion of workers were Black or Puerto Rican, including a forty-six-day strike beginning May 8, 1959. Cited from Cornell University Kheel Center.

endorsed by: Martin Luther King Jr. famously described Local 1199 as his favorite union, and Coretta Scott King later served as honorary chair of the union's organizing campaigns as 1199 expanded beyond New York. Cited from NYU Tamiment Library.

entities.historicalContext: 1199's hospital campaigns fused postwar civil-rights mobilization with industrial unionism in New York's voluntary hospitals; the union's 1930s Harlem pickets against segregated drugstores preceded decades of Black and Latina worker leadership in healthcare organizing across the Northeast.

entities.summary: At the 2007 U.S. Social Forum in Atlanta, more than fifty domestic workers from thirteen grassroots organizations voted to form the National Domestic Workers Alliance, creating a national body to advance dignity, standards, and organizing for nannies, house cleaners, and home care workers.

claims.object: The National Domestic Workers Alliance was founded at the U.S. Social Forum in Atlanta, Georgia in 2007 when domestic workers from thirteen organizations representing five states voted to form a national alliance
claims.citationLabel: Founded — National Domestic Workers Alliance

claims.object: Founding member organizations included Domestic Workers United and other New York, California, Maryland, and Bay Area groups that had spent years building local domestic-worker campaigns before the Atlanta gathering
claims.citationLabel: About NDWA

entities.locationLabel: Atlanta, Georgia (2007 U.S. Social Forum founding site)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: founded: The National Domestic Workers Alliance was founded at the U.S. Social Forum in Atlanta, Georgia in 2007 when domestic workers from thirteen organizations representing five states voted to form a national alliance. Cited from National Domestic Workers Alliance.

organized by: Founding member organizations included Domestic Workers United and other New York, California, Maryland, and Bay Area groups that had spent years building local domestic-worker campaigns before the Atlanta gathering. Cited from National Domestic Workers Alliance.

entities.historicalContext: NDWA extended a lineage from Dorothy Bolden's 1960s Atlanta domestic-worker organizing through state bill-of-rights campaigns in New York, California, and elsewhere; the Atlanta founding linked local worker centers into a national voice for a workforce historically excluded from federal labor protections.

entities.displayName: Thirteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution
entities.nameLower: thirteenth amendment to the u.s. constitution
entities.summary: Ratified December 6, 1865, the Thirteenth Amendment abolished slavery and involuntary servitude throughout the United States, except as punishment for crime, ending the legal institution that had held millions of Black Americans in bondage.

claims.object: December 6, 1865, when Georgia's ratification supplied the three-fourths of states required under Article V
claims.citationLabel: National Archives: 13th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution (1865)

claims.object: January 31, 1865, as a joint resolution, having failed in the House in 1864 before passing on the second attempt
claims.citationLabel: Congress.gov Constitution Annotated: Thirteenth Amendment

entities.locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: ratified on: December 6, 1865, when Georgia's ratification supplied the three-fourths of states required under Article V. Cited from archives.gov.
passed by congress on: January 31, 1865, as a joint resolution, having failed in the House in 1864 before passing on the second attempt. Cited from constitution.congress.gov.

entities.historicalContext: The Thirteenth Amendment was the first of the three Reconstruction Amendments and the first change to the Constitution in six decades. Lincoln pushed for its passage as a permanent constitutional settlement of slavery that the wartime Emancipation Proclamation, a military order limited to Confederate territory, could not by itself guarantee.

entities.displayName: Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution
entities.nameLower: fourteenth amendment to the u.s. constitution
entities.summary: Ratified July 9, 1868, the Fourteenth Amendment established birthright citizenship and guaranteed all persons equal protection and due process of law, becoming the constitutional foundation for nearly every 20th-century civil rights case.

claims.object: July 9, 1868
claims.citationLabel: National Archives: 14th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution (1868)

claims.object: that no state shall deprive any person of life, liberty, or property without due process of law, nor deny any person within its jurisdiction the equal protection of the laws
claims.citationLabel: Congress.gov Constitution Annotated: Fourteenth Amendment, Section 1

entities.locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: ratified on: July 9, 1868. Cited from archives.gov.

guarantees: that no state shall deprive any person of life, liberty, or property without due process of law, nor deny any person within its jurisdiction the equal protection of the laws. Cited from constitution.congress.gov.

entities.historicalContext: Section 1 overturned Dred Scott v. Sandford by making all persons born or naturalized in the United States citizens, and its Equal Protection and Due Process Clauses became the primary textual basis for Brown v. Board of Education, Loving v. Virginia, and most of the litigation cataloged in this wave.

entities.displayName: Fifteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution
entities.nameLower: fifteenth amendment to the u.s. constitution
entities.summary: Ratified February 3, 1870, the Fifteenth Amendment barred the federal and state governments from denying citizens the vote on account of race, color, or previous condition of servitude, enfranchising Black men after emancipation.

claims.object: February 3, 1870
claims.citationLabel: National Archives: 15th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution (1870)

claims.object: grandfather clauses, poll taxes, and literacy tests that Southern states adopted after Reconstruction and that the Supreme Court did not begin fully dismantling until Guinn v. United States (1915) and the Voting Rights Act of 1965
claims.citationLabel: Congress.gov Constitution Annotated: Fifteenth Amendment

entities.locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.
notabilityLabels: The entity (case, law, person) is tied to a judicial decision that set binding or widely cited precedent affecting Black Americans' rights or status.

The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: later undermined by: grandfather clauses, poll taxes, and literacy tests that Southern states adopted after Reconstruction and that the Supreme Court did not begin fully dismantling until Guinn v. United States (1915) and the Voting Rights Act of 1965. Cited from constitution.congress.gov.
ratified on: February 3, 1870. Cited from archives.gov.

entities.historicalContext: The last of the three Reconstruction Amendments, the Fifteenth was ratified as federal troops still occupied parts of the former Confederacy. Its promise went largely unenforced for nearly a century after Reconstruction's end in 1877, until the Voting Rights Act of 1965 gave it teeth through federal oversight.

entities.summary: Enacted April 9, 1866, over President Andrew Johnson's veto, this was the first federal law to declare all persons born in the United States citizens entitled to the same contract, property, and legal rights as white citizens.

claims.object: April 9, 1866, when the House voted 122-41 to override President Johnson's veto, following the Senate's override on April 6
claims.citationLabel: U.S. House of Representatives History, Art & Archives: The Civil Rights Bill of 1866

claims.object: 42 U.S.C. Sections 1981 and 1982, guaranteeing all citizens the same right to make contracts and to inherit, purchase, lease, and convey real and personal property as white citizens enjoy
claims.citationLabel: Cornell Legal Information Institute: 42 U.S.C. Section 1981

entities.locationLabel: United States Capitol, Washington, D.C.

entities.summary: Enacted March 2, 1867, over President Andrew Johnson's veto, this law divided the former Confederacy (except Tennessee) into five military districts and required states to write new constitutions guaranteeing Black male suffrage before rejoining the Union.

entities.summary: Enacted May 31, 1870, this first Enforcement Act made it a federal crime to use force, bribery, or intimidation to stop citizens from voting on account of race, giving federal prosecutors a tool against Klan violence targeting Black voters.

claims.object: May 31, 1870, empowering the President to enforce the Fifteenth Amendment and criminalizing interference with the right to vote

entities.summary: Signed March 3, 1865, this law created the Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands within the War Department to provide formerly enslaved people with food, medical care, education, and help negotiating labor contracts.

entities.historicalContext: The Bureau built thousands of schools for freedpeople -- seeding many historically Black colleges -- but chronic underfunding, a one-year charter later extended in 1866 over another Johnson veto, and violent local resistance limited its reach; Congress allowed it to lapse in 1872.

entities.summary: Signed July 26, 1948, President Truman's order declared equality of treatment in the armed forces regardless of race, color, religion, or national origin, launching the desegregation of the U.S. military that was substantially completed by 1954.

entities.historicalContext: Truman issued the order after the Army resisted integration and after Black veterans organized against continued segregation following World War II service; Secretary of the Army Kenneth Royall's refusal to comply led Truman to force his retirement, and full integration proceeded gradually through the Korean War.

entities.historicalContext: Mississippi was the first state to pass a Black Code after the Civil War, and South Carolina, Alabama, and Louisiana followed within weeks; the codes' vagrancy and labor-contract provisions were designed to reproduce plantation labor discipline under a new legal name, prompting Congress to respond with the Civil Rights Act of 1866 and, eventually, military Reconstruction.

entities.summary: Ratified December 19, 1865, South Carolina's Black Codes designated Black workers as "servants" and employers as "masters," imposed racially separate courts, and restricted occupations Black residents could pursue outside of farm and domestic labor.

Ferguson, cataloged separately in this wave, gave Jim Crow segregation its constitutional cover for the next six decades.

notabilityBasis.note: struck down by: the U.S. Supreme Court's unanimous Loving v. Virginia decision on June 12, 1967, which invalidated Virginia's ban and all remaining state anti-miscegenation laws. Cited from encyclopediavirginia.org.
signed on: March 20, 1924, by Governor E. Lee Trinkle, after the Senate passed it 23-4 and the House 72-9. Cited from encyclopediavirginia.org.

entities.historicalContext: The Act defined a "white person" as someone with no trace of non-Caucasian ancestry (with a narrow exception for descendants of Pocahontas), stripping legal identity from Virginians of mixed and Indigenous heritage as well as criminalizing interracial marriage. Richard and Mildred Loving were prosecuted under this law in 1958, and their case, cataloged separately in this wave, ended it and every other state's anti-miscegenation statute.

entities.summary: Decided March 6, 1857, the Supreme Court ruled 7-2 that Black Americans, enslaved or free, could never be U.S. citizens and that Congress had no power to ban slavery in the territories, a decision now widely regarded as the worst in the Court's history.

claims.object: March 6, 1857, 60 U.S. (19 How.) 393, in a 7-2 opinion written by Chief Justice Roger B. Taney
claims.citationLabel: National Archives: Dred Scott v. Sandford (1857)

claims.object: that people of African descent, whether enslaved or free, were not and could never become U.S. citizens, and that the Missouri Compromise's ban on slavery in northern territories was unconstitutional
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Dred Scott v. Sandford, 60 U.S. 393 (1857)

entities.locationLabel: St. Louis, Missouri (case originated in the St. Louis Circuit Court)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: March 6, 1857, 60 U.S. (19 How.) 393, in a 7-2 opinion written by Chief Justice Roger B. Taney. Cited from archives.gov.

held: that people of African descent, whether enslaved or free, were not and could never become U.S. citizens, and that the Missouri Compromise's ban on slavery in northern territories was unconstitutional. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

entities.historicalContext: Dred Scott had sued for his freedom based on residence in free territory. The ruling inflamed sectional tensions over slavery's expansion and is credited with accelerating the political collapse that led to the Civil War. It was nullified by the Thirteenth and Fourteenth Amendments, both cataloged in this wave.

entities.summary: Decided May 18, 1896, the Supreme Court upheld Louisiana's Separate Car Act 7-1, ruling that racially segregated facilities were constitutional if "separate but equal," a doctrine that legitimized Jim Crow segregation until Brown v. Board of Education in 1954.

claims.object: May 18, 1896, 163 U.S. 537, in a 7-1 decision written by Justice Henry Billings Brown, with Justice John Marshall Harlan dissenting alone
claims.citationLabel: National Archives: Plessy v. Ferguson (1896)

claims.object: Louisiana's Separate Car Act of 1890 as consistent with the Fourteenth Amendment, establishing the "separate but equal" doctrine for public facilities
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII Wex: Plessy v. Ferguson (1896)

entities.locationLabel: New Orleans, Louisiana (Homer Plessy's 1892 arrest site)
notabilityLabels: The entity (case, law, person) is tied to a judicial decision that set binding or widely cited precedent affecting Black Americans' rights or status.

The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: upheld: Louisiana's Separate Car Act of 1890 as consistent with the Fourteenth Amendment, establishing the "separate but equal" doctrine for public facilities. Cited from law.cornell.edu.
decided on: May 18, 1896, 163 U.S. 537, in a 7-1 decision written by Justice Henry Billings Brown, with Justice John Marshall Harlan dissenting alone. Cited from archives.gov.

entities.historicalContext: Homer Plessy, a man of mixed racial ancestry classified as Black under Louisiana law, boarded a whites-only railcar in 1892 as part of a planned test case organized by New Orleans's Comite des Citoyens. The ruling provided constitutional cover for de jure segregation across the South for the next 58 years, until Brown v. Board of Education overturned it.

entities.displayName: Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka
entities.nameLower: brown v. board of education of topeka
entities.summary: Decided May 17, 1954, the Supreme Court unanimously ruled that racially segregated public schools violate the Fourteenth Amendment's Equal Protection Clause even where facilities are physically equal, overturning Plessy v. Ferguson's separate-but-equal doctrine in education.

claims.object: May 17, 1954, 347 U.S. 483, in a unanimous 9-0 opinion written by Chief Justice Earl Warren
claims.citationLabel: National Archives: Brown v. Board of Education (1954)

claims.object: that "separate educational facilities are inherently unequal" and that state-mandated school segregation violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (1954)

entities.locationLabel: Monroe Elementary School (now Brown v. Board of Education National Historical Park), Topeka, Kansas
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: May 17, 1954, 347 U.S. 483, in a unanimous 9-0 opinion written by Chief Justice Earl Warren. Cited from archives.gov.

held: that "separate educational facilities are inherently unequal" and that state-mandated school segregation violates the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

entities.historicalContext: The case consolidated challenges from Kansas, South Carolina, Virginia, Delaware, and the District of Columbia (the D.C. companion case, Bolling v. Sharpe, is cataloged separately). Thurgood Marshall argued the case for the NAACP Legal Defense Fund. The Court did not order specific relief until Brown II (1955), and Cooper v. Aaron (1958) later reaffirmed that states could not evade the ruling.

entities.summary: Decided the same day as Brown v. Board, this unanimous 1954 ruling held that segregated public schools in Washington, D.C. violate the Fifth Amendment's Due Process Clause, since the Fourteenth Amendment's Equal Protection Clause does not apply to the federal government directly.

claims.object: May 17, 1954, 347 U.S. 497, unanimously, in an opinion by Chief Justice Earl Warren
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Bolling v. Sharpe, 347 U.S. 497 (1954)

claims.object: that racial segregation in the District of Columbia's public schools denies Black students due process of law under the Fifth Amendment, since discrimination "may be so unjustifiable as to be violative of due process"
claims.citationLabel: DC History Center: Bolling v. Sharpe, 70 Years Later

entities.locationLabel: United States Supreme Court Building, Washington, D.C.
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: May 17, 1954, 347 U.S. 497, unanimously, in an opinion by Chief Justice Earl Warren. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

held: that racial segregation in the District of Columbia's public schools denies Black students due process of law under the Fifth Amendment, since discrimination "may be so unjustifiable as to be violative of due process". Cited from dchistory.org.

entities.historicalContext: Because the Fifth Amendment, unlike the Fourteenth, contains no equal protection clause and applies to the federal government rather than the states, the Court read an equal-protection component into the Fifth Amendment's due process guarantee -- a doctrine now known as "reverse incorporation" that later applied equal-protection-style scrutiny to federal action generally.

entities.summary: Decided unanimously June 12, 1967, the Supreme Court struck down Virginia's Racial Integrity Act and all state bans on interracial marriage as violations of the Equal Protection and Due Process Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment.

claims.object: June 12, 1967, 388 U.S. 1, unanimously, in an opinion written by Chief Justice Earl Warren
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Loving v. Virginia, 388 U.S. 1 (1967)

claims.object: that Virginia's ban on interracial marriage under the Racial Integrity Act of 1924 violated the Equal Protection and Due Process Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment, invalidating anti-miscegenation laws in the sixteen states that still had them
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia Virginia: Loving v. Virginia (June 12, 1967)

entities.locationLabel: Caroline County Courthouse area, Bowling Green, Virginia
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: June 12, 1967, 388 U.S. 1, unanimously, in an opinion written by Chief Justice Earl Warren. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

held: that Virginia's ban on interracial marriage under the Racial Integrity Act of 1924 violated the Equal Protection and Due Process Clauses of the Fourteenth Amendment, invalidating anti-miscegenation laws in the sixteen states that still had them. Cited from encyclopediavirginia.org.

entities.historicalContext: Richard and Mildred Loving married in Washington, D.C. in 1958 to evade Virginia's law, then were arrested after returning to Caroline County; police found them in bed together and treated their marriage certificate as evidence of a crime. The couple accepted a suspended sentence conditioned on leaving Virginia for 25 years before the ACLU took their case to the Supreme Court.

entities.summary: Decided May 3, 1948, the Supreme Court ruled that while private racially restrictive covenants are not themselves unconstitutional, state courts cannot constitutionally enforce them, since judicial enforcement constitutes discriminatory state action barred by the Fourteenth Amendment.

claims.object: May 3, 1948, 334 U.S. 1, in a 6-0 decision (three justices recused) written by Chief Justice Fred Vinson
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII Wex: Shelley v. Kraemer (1948)

claims.object: that judicial enforcement of a racially restrictive covenant by a state court constitutes state action that violates the Equal Protection Clause, even though the covenant itself was a private agreement
claims.citationLabel: Library of Congress: U.S. Reports -- Shelley v. Kraemer, 334 U.S. 1 (1948)

entities.locationLabel: St. Louis, Missouri (the Shelley family's home on Labadie Avenue)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: May 3, 1948, 334 U.S. 1, in a 6-0 decision (three justices recused) written by Chief Justice Fred Vinson. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

held: that judicial enforcement of a racially restrictive covenant by a state court constitutes state action that violates the Equal Protection Clause, even though the covenant itself was a private agreement. Cited from loc.gov.

entities.historicalContext: J.D. and Ethel Shelley, a Black family, purchased a St. Louis home in 1945 without knowing a 1911 covenant barred occupancy by "people of the Negro or Mongolian Race." The ruling built on Corrigan v. Buckley (1926), cataloged in this wave, which had upheld such covenants two decades earlier, and set the stage for later fair-housing litigation including Jones v. Alfred H. Mayer Co. (1968).

entities.summary: Decided unanimously June 5, 1950, the Supreme Court ordered the University of Texas Law School to admit Heman Sweatt, ruling that a hastily created separate Black law school could not offer an equal legal education, a key precursor to Brown v. Board of Education.

claims.object: June 5, 1950, 339 U.S. 629, unanimously, in an opinion by Chief Justice Fred Vinson
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Sweatt v. Painter, 339 U.S. 629 (1950)

claims.object: the University of Texas Law School's 16 full-time faculty, 850 students, and 65,000-volume library against the makeshift Black law school's 5 faculty, 23 students, and 16,500 volumes, finding the two schools not substantially equal
claims.citationLabel: Tarlton Law Library, University of Texas: Sweatt v. Painter (1950)

entities.locationLabel: University of Texas School of Law, Austin, Texas
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: June 5, 1950, 339 U.S. 629, unanimously, in an opinion by Chief Justice Fred Vinson. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

compared: the University of Texas Law School's 16 full-time faculty, 850 students, and 65,000-volume library against the makeshift Black law school's 5 faculty, 23 students, and 16,500 volumes, finding the two schools not substantially equal. Cited from tarlton.law.utexas.edu.

entities.historicalContext: Heman Marion Sweatt, a Houston postal worker, applied to the University of Texas Law School in 1946 and was rejected solely because he was Black; the state stalled by creating a separate law school in Houston rather than integrate. Decided the same day as McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents, both cataloged in this wave, Sweatt signaled the Court's growing willingness to scrutinize whether segregated graduate programs were truly equal, four years before Brown.

entities.displayName: Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada
entities.nameLower: missouri ex rel. gaines v. canada
entities.summary: Decided December 12, 1938, the Supreme Court ruled that a state providing a law school for white students must provide equivalent in-state legal education to Black students too, rather than merely paying their tuition to attend school in another state.

claims.object: December 12, 1938, 305 U.S. 337, in a majority opinion by Chief Justice Charles Evans Hughes
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada, 305 U.S. 337 (1938)

claims.object: that Missouri's practice of paying Black applicants' tuition to attend law school in an adjacent state, rather than admitting them to the University of Missouri, failed to provide equal protection since it created no in-state option at all
claims.citationLabel: African American Registry: Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada is Decided

entities.locationLabel: University of Missouri School of Law, Columbia, Missouri
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: December 12, 1938, 305 U.S. 337, in a majority opinion by Chief Justice Charles Evans Hughes. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

held: that Missouri's practice of paying Black applicants' tuition to attend law school in an adjacent state, rather than admitting them to the University of Missouri, failed to provide equal protection since it created no in-state option at all. Cited from aaregistry.org.

entities.historicalContext: Lloyd Gaines, denied admission to the University of Missouri's law school in Columbia because he was Black, sued registrar Silas Woodson Canada. The NAACP Legal Defense Fund's first major graduate-school victory, the case established the legal template later used in Sipuel, McLaurin, and Sweatt (all cataloged in this wave) before Brown v. Board dismantled segregated education entirely.

entities.summary: Decided April 3, 1944, the Supreme Court ruled 8-1 that the Texas Democratic Party's whites-only primary election constituted unconstitutional state action, ending the all-white primary as a tool of Black voter disenfranchisement across the South.

claims.object: April 3, 1944, 321 U.S. 649, in an 8-1 decision written by Justice Stanley Reed
claims.citationLabel: NAACP Legal Defense Fund: Landmark Case -- Smith v. Allwright

claims.object: that excluding Black voters from the Texas Democratic Party's primary election violated the Fifteenth Amendment because primaries were an integral part of the state's election machinery, making the party's whites-only rule state action
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Smith v. Allwright, 321 U.S. 649 (1944)

entities.locationLabel: Harris County, Houston, Texas
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

The entity is documented as the only or oldest surviving example of its kind in a defined scope (oldest Black-owned business in a city, only remaining Rosenwald school in a county) with evidence supporting the superlative claim.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: April 3, 1944, 321 U.S. 649, in an 8-1 decision written by Justice Stanley Reed. Cited from naacpldf.org.

held: that excluding Black voters from the Texas Democratic Party's primary election violated the Fifteenth Amendment because primaries were an integral part of the state's election machinery, making the party's whites-only rule state action. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

entities.historicalContext: Lonnie Smith, a Black voter in Harris County, sued election official S.S. Allwright after being denied a primary ballot. Within four years, registered Black voters across the South rose from about 200,000 to 800,000. Terry v. Adams (1953), cataloged in this wave, later closed the remaining loophole of an unofficial "pre-primary" used to evade this ruling.

entities.summary: Decided June 21, 1915, the Supreme Court unanimously struck down Oklahoma's "grandfather clause," which exempted from a new literacy test anyone entitled to vote in 1866, before Black men gained suffrage, ruling it an unconstitutional evasion of the Fifteenth Amendment.

claims.object: June 21, 1915, 238 U.S. 347, unanimously (8-0), in an opinion by Chief Justice Edward Douglass White
claims.citationLabel: Encyclopedia of Oklahoma History and Culture: Guinn v. United States (1915)

claims.object: that Oklahoma's grandfather clause, exempting from a literacy test all descendants of those entitled to vote before the Fifteenth Amendment's 1870 ratification, was "repugnant to the Fifteenth Amendment and therefore null and void"
claims.citationLabel: BlackPast.org: Guinn v. United States (1915)

entities.locationLabel: Oklahoma (statewide grandfather clause prosecuted against Muskogee County election officials)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: June 21, 1915, 238 U.S. 347, unanimously (8-0), in an opinion by Chief Justice Edward Douglass White. Cited from okhistory.org.

held: that Oklahoma's grandfather clause, exempting from a literacy test all descendants of those entitled to vote before the Fifteenth Amendment's 1870 ratification, was "repugnant to the Fifteenth Amendment and therefore null and void". Cited from blackpast.org.

entities.historicalContext: Despite the ruling, Oklahoma immediately adopted a new registration scheme requiring anyone who had not voted in 1914 to register within a narrow window or lose the franchise permanently, showing how quickly Southern legislatures replaced one disenfranchising device with another -- a pattern the Voting Rights Act of 1965, cataloged in this wave, was eventually designed to break.

entities.summary: Decided unanimously November 5, 1917, the Supreme Court struck down a Louisville ordinance banning the sale of property to Black buyers in majority-white blocks, ruling that municipal residential segregation ordinances violate the Fourteenth Amendment's property rights.

claims.object: November 5, 1917, 245 U.S. 60, unanimously, in an opinion by Justice William R. Day
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Buchanan v. Warley, 245 U.S. 60 (1917)

claims.object: that a Louisville ordinance barring Black residents from buying homes on blocks with a white majority (and vice versa) violated the Fourteenth Amendment's protection of the right to buy and sell property, regardless of the races involved
claims.citationLabel: Zinn Education Project: Supreme Court Rules Against Segregated Housing (1917)

entities.locationLabel: Louisville, Kentucky
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: November 5, 1917, 245 U.S. 60, unanimously, in an opinion by Justice William R. Day. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

held: that a Louisville ordinance barring Black residents from buying homes on blocks with a white majority (and vice versa) violated the Fourteenth Amendment's protection of the right to buy and sell property, regardless of the races involved. Cited from zinnedproject.org.

entities.historicalContext: NAACP attorney and prospective Black buyer William Warley arranged the test case with white seller Charles Buchanan specifically to challenge the 1914 ordinance. Though it ended explicit municipal zoning segregation, cities and private parties soon turned to racially restrictive covenants -- upheld in Corrigan v. Buckley (1926) and not barred until Shelley v. Kraemer (1948), both cataloged in this wave -- to achieve similar ends.

entities.summary: Decided November 7, 1932, the Supreme Court reversed the convictions of the Scottsboro Boys, ruling that capital defendants who cannot afford counsel and cannot adequately defend themselves must be given effective legal representation appointed in time to prepare a defense.

claims.object: November 7, 1932, 287 U.S. 45, reversing the convictions in a majority opinion by Justice George Sutherland
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Powell v. Alabama, 287 U.S. 45 (1932)

claims.object: that in capital cases, a defendant too poor or incapacitated to secure counsel must have counsel appointed with enough time to investigate and prepare a defense, as a requirement of Fourteenth Amendment due process
claims.citationLabel: Justia U.S. Supreme Court Center: Powell v. Alabama, 287 U.S. 45 (1932)

entities.locationLabel: Jackson County Courthouse, Scottsboro, Alabama
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: November 7, 1932, 287 U.S. 45, reversing the convictions in a majority opinion by Justice George Sutherland. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

held: that in capital cases, a defendant too poor or incapacitated to secure counsel must have counsel appointed with enough time to investigate and prepare a defense, as a requirement of Fourteenth Amendment due process. Cited from supreme.justia.com.

entities.historicalContext: Nine Black teenagers, later known as the Scottsboro Boys, were accused of raping two white women aboard a freight train and were tried within days without meaningful access to counsel, receiving death sentences after one-day trials. The case was the first time the Supreme Court reversed a state conviction for violating a Bill of Rights criminal procedure guarantee; Norris v. Alabama, cataloged in this wave, addressed jury discrimination in the same prosecutions three years later.

entities.summary: Decided April 30, 1986, the Supreme Court ruled 7-2 that prosecutors cannot use peremptory jury challenges to strike potential jurors solely because of their race, establishing the "Batson challenge" that defendants still use today to contest discriminatory jury selection.

claims.object: April 30, 1986, 476 U.S. 79, in a 7-2 decision written by Justice Lewis Powell
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Batson v. Kentucky, 476 U.S. 79 (1986)

claims.object: that a prosecutor's use of peremptory challenges to strike jurors based solely on race violates the Equal Protection Clause, and established a three-step test for defendants to challenge such strikes
claims.citationLabel: U.S. Courts: Facts and Case Summary -- Batson v. Kentucky

entities.locationLabel: Jefferson County, Louisville, Kentucky
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: April 30, 1986, 476 U.S. 79, in a 7-2 decision written by Justice Lewis Powell. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

held: that a prosecutor's use of peremptory challenges to strike jurors based solely on race violates the Equal Protection Clause, and established a three-step test for defendants to challenge such strikes. Cited from uscourts.gov.

entities.historicalContext: At James Batson's Louisville burglary trial, the prosecutor used peremptory strikes to remove all four Black prospective jurors, leaving an all-white jury. The ruling reached only prosecutors' strikes at first; later cases extended the rule to defense attorneys and to civil litigants.

entities.displayName: Regents of the University of California v. Bakke
entities.nameLower: regents of the university of california v. bakke
entities.summary: Decided June 28, 1978, a fractured Supreme Court struck down a medical school's fixed racial quota system while ruling that race could still be considered as one factor among many in university admissions, shaping affirmative-action law for the next 45 years.

claims.object: June 28, 1978, 438 U.S. 265, with six separate opinions and no single majority rationale; Justice Lewis Powell's controlling opinion allowed race as one admissions factor but barred fixed quotas
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII Wex: Regents of the University of California v. Bakke (1978)

claims.object: UC Davis Medical School's policy of reserving 16 of 100 admission slots exclusively for minority applicants, ordering Allan Bakke's admission
claims.citationLabel: National Constitution Center: Regents of the University of California v. Bakke (1978)

entities.locationLabel: University of California, Davis School of Medicine, Davis, California
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: June 28, 1978, 438 U.S. 265, with six separate opinions and no single majority rationale; Justice Lewis Powell's controlling opinion allowed race as one admissions factor but barred fixed quotas. Cited from law.cornell.edu.
struck down: UC Davis Medical School's policy of reserving 16 of 100 admission slots exclusively for minority applicants, ordering Allan Bakke's admission. Cited from constitutioncenter.org.

entities.historicalContext: Bakke, a white applicant rejected twice despite higher test scores than some admitted minority applicants, sued after UC Davis set aside a fixed number of seats for minority students. The decision's holding that diversity could justify some use of race in admissions stood until Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard (2023), cataloged in this wave, overruled it.

entities.displayName: Green v. County School Board of New Kent County
entities.nameLower: green v. county school board of new kent county
entities.summary: Decided unanimously May 27, 1968, the Supreme Court rejected "freedom of choice" school desegregation plans as inadequate, ruling that school boards have an affirmative duty to dismantle segregated systems "root and branch" rather than merely stop enforcing segregation.

claims.object: May 27, 1968, 391 U.S. 430, unanimously, in an opinion by Justice William Brennan
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Green v. County School Board of New Kent County, 391 U.S. 430 (1968)

claims.object: that New Kent County's freedom-of-choice plan, under which no white student had chosen the Black school in three years, did not satisfy the school board's affirmative duty to eliminate segregation "root and branch"
claims.citationLabel: National Archives Education Blog: Green v. New Kent County

entities.locationLabel: New Kent County, Virginia
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: May 27, 1968, 391 U.S. 430, unanimously, in an opinion by Justice William Brennan. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

held: that New Kent County's freedom-of-choice plan, under which no white student had chosen the Black school in three years, did not satisfy the school board's affirmative duty to eliminate segregation "root and branch". Cited from education.blogs.archives.gov.

entities.historicalContext: New Kent County operated two schools, one historically for white students and one for Black students, and let families choose between them; after three years, 85 percent of Black students still attended the Black school and no white student had transferred. The ruling's "Green factors" -- faculty, staff, transportation, facilities, and extracurriculars -- became the standard courts used to judge desegregation progress until Milliken v. Bradley (1974), cataloged in this wave, limited how far remedies could reach.

entities.displayName: Heart of Atlanta Motel, Inc. v. United States
entities.nameLower: heart of atlanta motel, inc. v. united states
entities.summary: Decided unanimously December 14, 1964, the Supreme Court upheld Title II of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, ruling that Congress's Commerce Clause power let it ban racial discrimination in public accommodations serving interstate travelers.

claims.object: December 14, 1964, 379 U.S. 241, unanimously, in an opinion by Justice Tom C. Clark
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Heart of Atlanta Motel v. United States, 379 U.S. 241 (1964)

claims.object: that Congress's Commerce Clause power extends to regulating a local motel that refuses Black guests, because such discrimination in the aggregate substantially burdens interstate travel and commerce
claims.citationLabel: Tarlton Law Library, University of Texas: Heart of Atlanta Motel v. United States (1964)

entities.locationLabel: Heart of Atlanta Motel site, downtown Atlanta, Georgia
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: December 14, 1964, 379 U.S. 241, unanimously, in an opinion by Justice Tom C. Clark. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

held: that Congress's Commerce Clause power extends to regulating a local motel that refuses Black guests, because such discrimination in the aggregate substantially burdens interstate travel and commerce. Cited from tarlton.law.utexas.edu.

entities.historicalContext: The motel's owner, who restricted rooms to white patrons, sued to block enforcement of the newly passed Civil Rights Act, arguing Congress had exceeded its power. Decided the same day as its companion case Katzenbach v. McClung, cataloged in this wave, the ruling foreclosed the primary constitutional challenge to the Act's public-accommodations provisions before they took effect nationwide.

entities.summary: Decided March 7, 1966, the Supreme Court upheld the Voting Rights Act of 1965 by an 8-1 vote, ruling that its preclearance requirement for jurisdictions with histories of voting discrimination was a valid exercise of Congress's power to enforce the Fifteenth Amendment.

claims.object: March 7, 1966, 383 U.S. 301, in an 8-1 decision written by Chief Justice Earl Warren
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: South Carolina v. Katzenbach, 383 U.S. 301 (1966)

claims.object: the Voting Rights Act's Section 4 coverage formula and Section 5 preclearance requirement against South Carolina's claims that they violated states' rights and equal state sovereignty
claims.citationLabel: National Constitution Center: South Carolina v. Katzenbach (1966)

entities.locationLabel: Columbia, South Carolina (South Carolina was the plaintiff state challenging the Act)
notabilityLabels: The entity (case, law, person) is tied to a judicial decision that set binding or widely cited precedent affecting Black Americans' rights or status.

The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: upheld: the Voting Rights Act's Section 4 coverage formula and Section 5 preclearance requirement against South Carolina's claims that they violated states' rights and equal state sovereignty. Cited from constitutioncenter.org.
decided on: March 7, 1966, 383 U.S. 301, in an 8-1 decision written by Chief Justice Earl Warren. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

entities.historicalContext: South Carolina sued U.S. Attorney General Nicholas Katzenbach directly, challenging the Voting Rights Act as an unconstitutional bill of attainder against Southern states. The ruling secured the Act's core enforcement tools for nearly five decades until Shelby County v. Holder (2013), cataloged in this wave, invalidated the coverage formula this decision had upheld.

entities.summary: Decided September 12, 1958, in an opinion signed individually by all nine justices, the Supreme Court ruled that Arkansas officials could not delay or evade school desegregation ordered under Brown v. Board, affirming that state officials are bound by federal constitutional rulings.

claims.object: September 12, 1958, 358 U.S. 1, in an opinion jointly and individually signed by all nine justices, primarily drafted by Justice William Brennan
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Cooper v. Aaron, 358 U.S. 1 (1958)

claims.object: that the Little Rock School Board could not delay desegregation for 30 months in response to Governor Orval Faubus's violent resistance, and that Brown v. Board's constitutional interpretation binds all state officials under the Supremacy Clause
claims.citationLabel: Teaching American History: Cooper v. Aaron (1958)

entities.locationLabel: Little Rock Central High School, Little Rock, Arkansas
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: September 12, 1958, 358 U.S. 1, in an opinion jointly and individually signed by all nine justices, primarily drafted by Justice William Brennan. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

held: that the Little Rock School Board could not delay desegregation for 30 months in response to Governor Orval Faubus's violent resistance, and that Brown v. Board's constitutional interpretation binds all state officials under the Supremacy Clause. Cited from teachingamericanhistory.org.

entities.historicalContext: Governor Faubus deployed the Arkansas National Guard in 1957 to block the Little Rock Nine from entering Central High School, prompting President Eisenhower to federalize the Guard and send the 101st Airborne. The unprecedented joint-signature opinion was the Court's emphatic response to Southern states' "massive resistance" strategy of defying Brown.

entities.summary: Decided 5-4 on June 25, 2013, the Supreme Court struck down the Voting Rights Act's coverage formula for determining which jurisdictions needed federal preclearance of election changes, effectively suspending the Act's preclearance enforcement mechanism.

claims.object: June 25, 2013, 570 U.S. 529, in a 5-4 decision written by Chief Justice John Roberts
claims.citationLabel: Cornell LII: Shelby County v. Holder, 570 U.S. 529 (2013)

claims.object: that the Voting Rights Act's Section 4(b) coverage formula was unconstitutional because it relied on decades-old data, effectively disabling Section 5 preclearance since no formula remained to determine which jurisdictions it covered
claims.citationLabel: Brennan Center for Justice: Effects of Shelby County v. Holder on the Voting Rights Act

entities.locationLabel: Shelby County, Alabama (county seat: Columbiana)
notabilityLabels: The entity is a documented site of a historically significant event or practice (a sit-in lunch counter, a Freedom School, a documented station on the Underground Railroad) with primary-source evidence tying the site to the event.

notabilityBasis.note: decided on: June 25, 2013, 570 U.S. 529, in a 5-4 decision written by Chief Justice John Roberts. Cited from law.cornell.edu.

held: that the Voting Rights Act's Section 4(b) coverage formula was unconstitutional because it relied on decades-old data, effectively disabling Section 5 preclearance since no formula remained to determine which jurisdictions it covered. Cited from brennancenter.org.
